title: 3/29 @ 1PM - Sunday Games at Sketchbook Evanston
description:
Evanston Board Games Meetup
Join us at Sketchbook Brewing Company in Evanston for our Sunday Board Game Meetup!

We meet most Sundays at Sketchbook. Just look for the group in the back that has stacks of board games to pick from! We welcome people of all skill levels to come and play, as we always teach new games to people that have not played them before.

Feel free to bring your own games that you want to teach and are familiar with. A typical event starts with talking about what we brought that week, and then we break out into smaller groups to play games based on player count and what everyone is interested in. It's common to have 2 or 3 games going at once, and fitting in a second or third game before the event ends at 4pm. Please RSVP and be on time so that we can include you at the start of the first game, and buy a drink to support the venue!

title: #113: The Seven Daughters of Dupree by Nikesha Elise Williams
description:
Black Girls Read Book Club
**Our March book selection is *The Seven Daughters of Dupree* by Chicago native Nikesha Elise Williams!** We will meet at [Zora's Place](https://www.zorasplace.com/), Evanston's only Black woman-owned bookshop.

About the book: It’s 1995, and fourteen-year-old Tati is determined to uncover the identity of her father. But her mother, Nadia, keeps her secrets close, while her grandmother Gladys remains silent about the family’s past, including why she left Land’s End, Alabama, in 1953. As Tati digs deeper, she uncovers a legacy of family secrets, where every generation of Dupree women has posed more questions than answers.

From Jubi in 1917, whose attempt to pass for white ends when she gives birth to Ruby; to Ruby’s fiery lust for Sampson in 1934 that leads to a baby of her own; to the night in 1980 that changed Nadia’s future forever, the Dupree women carry the weight of their heritage. Bound by a mysterious malediction that means they will only give birth to daughters, the Dupree women confront a legacy of pain, resilience, and survival that began with an enslaved ancestor who risked everything for freedom.

*The Seven Daughters of Dupree* masterfully weaves together themes of generational trauma, Black women’s resilience, and unbreakable familial bonds. Nikesha Elise Williams delivers a feminist literary fiction that explores the ripple effects of actions, secrets, and love through seven generations of Black women.

***The Seven Daughters of Dupree* will be released in January 2026 and is available for pre-order now. Support Zora's Place by purchasing your book at [their brick-and-mortar storefront](https://www.zorasplace.com/) or [their online storefront on Bookshop](https://bookshop.org/a/5326/9781668051948).**

title: Music Theater Works: Cats
description:
Music and Lyrics by Andrew Lloyd Webber
Based on “Old Possum’s Book of Practical Cats” by T.S. Eliot
Directed and Choreographed by Mandy Modic
Music Directed by Linda Madonia
Music Theater Works kicks off its 46th season with CATS, Andrew Lloyd Webber’s electrifying, Tony Award-winning musical. In this immersive production set in a larger-than-life British flat, audiences may join the magical and all encompassing world of the Jellicle cats as they gather for the annual Jellicle Ball, where one will be chosen for a new life. Through a series of dazzling musical numbers, each cat shares their story— from the mischievous Rum Tum Tugger, to the mysterious Macavity, and the once-glamorous Grizabella. Featuring unforgettable songs, breathtaking choreography, and a whimsical atmosphere, CATS is a celebration of life, community and the power of memory

title: Monday Matinees
location: 4000 W. Pratt Ave., Lincolnwood, IL, 60712, US
description: Enjoy great films at your library every Monday! Doors open at 12:45pm.  Father of the Bride (1991)Mon, Mar 2 • 1-2:45pm • PG, 100 mins •  [D]In this remake of the 1950 comedy, a frazzled shoe executive tries to cope with the financial and emotional costs of his only daughter's impending marriage while also dealing with an eccentric wedding coordinator. **Paulie (1998)Mon, Mar 9 • 1-2:45pm • PG, 91 mins •  [D]A chatty parrot named Paulie recalls his search to find the little girl who first owned him and gets help from a kind janitor. Wicked: For Good (2025)Mon, Mar 23 • 1-3:30pm • PG, 138 mins •  [D]Now demonized as the Wicked Witch of the West, Elphaba lives in exile in the Ozian forest, while Glinda resides at the palace in Emerald City, reveling in the perks of fame and popularity. As an angry mob rises against the Wicked Witch, she'll need to reunite with Glinda to transform herself, and all of Oz, for good. The Long Walk (2025)Mon, Mar 30 • 1-3pm • R, 108 mins •  [D]In a dystopian near-future, 100 teenage boys must embark on an annual competitive test known as "The Long Walk." The rules are simple: maintain a speed above 4 miles per hour. Receive three warnings in an hour and you're shot dead. The last one walking gets whatever he wants for the rest of his life. Under these grim circumstances, the boys develop deep friendships despite knowing that each of their friends' survival is a threat to their own. There will be no Monday Matinee on March 16 due to the library serving as a polling location.

title: Introduction to News Media Literacy
description: Our media ecosystem today is increasingly messy. It's a mix of professionally produced and user-generated content that often blurs together on platforms like YouTube, Facebook, and TikTok. The rapid rise of artificial intelligence has added yet another layer of complexity, making it even easier for misleading or fabricated material to appear credible. Michael A. Spikes of Northwestern University’s Medill School of Journalism explores how this blending of sources—and now AI-generated material—creates a “blurring of the lines” between types of content, making it harder to know what to trust. You'll gain practical skills for assessing the credibility of information from different outlets and strategies for building a set of reliable sources you can return to regularly.Michael Spikes, Ph.D., has been teaching, writing about, and developing curriculum on news media literacy for more than 15 years. Currently, he is a professor of practice and the program director of Teach for Chicago Journalism at the Medill School of Journalism at Northwestern University. Before joining Northwestern, Michael was a project manager for the Center for News Literacy at Stony Brook University. This event is part of the Civic Empowerment Series 2026, in partnership with Schaumburg Township District Library, Vernon Area Public Library, Blue Island Public Library, Highland Park Public Library, Aurora Public Library District, La Grange Public Library, Fox River Valley Public Library, Algonquin Area Public Library District, and Oak Park Public Library. Register and receive a Zoom link for this event.For questions about this event, call Schaumburg Township District Library at 847-923-3347.

title: Navigating News Media with Michael Spikes [Z]
description: Join us for a virtual session of the Civic Empowerment Series 2026, presented in partnership with Schaumburg Public Library and other area libraries. Dr. Michael A. Spikes of Northwestern University’s Medill School of Journalism will explain how today’s media landscape—shaped by social platforms, mixed information sources, and AI-generated content—can make it difficult to know what to trust. Learn practical strategies for evaluating credibility and building a reliable set of news sources you can use with confidence.The Civic Empowerment Series 2026 is brought to you in partnership with Schaumburg Public Library, Skokie Public Library, Vernon Area Public Library, Blue Island Public Library, Highland Park Public Library, Aurora Public Library District, La Grange Public Library, Fox River Valley Public Library, Algonquin Area Public Library District, Roselle Public Library and Oak Park Public Library. Please note: On the Schaumburg Township District Library registration page, please select online. Schaumburg Township District Library will email a Zoom link three hours prior to the start of the program.This program will take place on Zoom.Register Here!

title: The Alamo and The Republic of Tejas
description: This presentation reexamines the Alamo and the Republic of Tejas, cutting through familiar myths with historical context and turning a sharp eye toward American legend-making. It explores how rebellion, expansion, and selective memory shaped nationalism, borders, and Texan culture—often at Mexico’s expense. The Alamo, The Republic of Tejas, and Never Forgetting invites audiences to remember the past a little less comfortably, and a lot more honestly.

title: Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
The storied city of Djenné, a center of Islamic learning, study, and scholarship since the twelfth century, is the hometown of Bamako-based photographer Hamdia Traoré (b. 1992, Mali). The thirty portraits in Traore’s series Des marabouts de Djenné (Marabouts of Jenne) reflect his intimate connections to the city’s people and deep history. Learned and devout, marabouts teach in Djenné’s over 50 Qur’anic schools, offer spiritual guidance, and treat ailments through their knowledge of the Qur’an.
Made during a time of political and social upheaval in Mali, these portraits reflect enduring cultural resilience. Each image depicts a marabout seated with the tools of his practice—books, Qur’an boards, amulets, and prayer beads—framed by the architecture and atmosphere of Djenné. The consistent format underscores their collective identity, while individual poses and captions highlight personal roles and neighborhoods.
Traoré’s work will be shown alongside mid-20th-century black-and-white portraits of marabouts by Malian photographers Mamadou Cissé, Abdourahmane Sakaly, and Tijani Sitou. Drawn from the Archive of Malian Photography, these earlier images share visual parallels and deepen the historical context. Together, these images illuminate evolving perspectives on spiritual authority, identity, and visual representation in Mali.

Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali” is curated by Kathleen Bickford Berzock, Associate Director of Curatorial Affairs, The Block Museum of Art, Northwestern University, in collaboration with Candace M. Keller, Associate Professor, Art History & Visual Culture, Michigan State University.  We are grateful to the Archive of Malian Photography (https://amp.matrix.msu.edu/), which provided digital scans of archival works. At The Block, the exhibition is supported by the Kadin/Spiegel Family Endowed Fund and a grant from the Illinois Arts Council.

title: Teresa Montoya’s Tó Łitso (Yellow Water): Ten Years after the Gold King Mine Spill
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
On August 5, 2015, the rupture of the abandoned Gold King Mine near Silverton, Colorado, released more than three million gallons of toxic wastewater into the Animas River, turning its waters a shocking shade of yellow. In the following year, artist and anthropologist Teresa Montoya (Diné, born 1984) embarked on a road trip from Silverton to Shiprock, New Mexico, retracing the path of the contaminated water and documenting its ongoing cultural, spiritual, and material effects on the Navajo Nation and other Indigenous communities downstream.
Marking the ten-year anniversary of the disaster, The Block Museum of Art partners with Montoya to revisit this journey. Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) explores the enduring consequences of the Gold King Mine spill through photography, sound recordings, water samples, and cartographic data.
Combining documentary photographs with scientific data and poetic reflection, Tó Łitso invites viewers to consider water not only as a life-sustaining resource but also as a conduit for histories, stories, and harm. In doing so, the exhibition challenges extractive frameworks of land use, centering Indigenous knowledge and resilience. Through Montoya’s interdisciplinary practice, Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) offers a powerful meditation on environmental and cultural justice in the Southwest and beyond.
"The 2015 spill had discharged more than three million gallons of acidic mine waste fluid into Cement Creek before joining the Animas River and eventually into the San Juan River, which flows directly across the Navajo Nation. Through my photographic journey, I make explicit the enduring presence of toxicity across multiple landscapes and territories. Sometimes it appears beautiful, other times haunting. The images highlight the relationships that various communities sustain through water/tó despite the occurrence of repeated and enduring contamination from upstream locales. The Gold King Mine Spill shows us this, even in that, which cannot be readily seen." – Teresa Montoya

title: Religious Studies Speaker Series: Brett Hendrickson
description:
Miraculous Earth: The Santuario de Chimayó

Northern New Mexico boasts dozens of colonial-era adobe churches, most built by Pueblo and Hispano hands under the watchful guidance of Spanish Franciscan friars. Each is special in its own way, but one stands out among the rest: the Santuario de Chimayó, located in the village of Chimayó. What makes this so unique? In one of its side chapels, there is a hole in the stone floor, opening onto the sandy earth. Hundreds of thousands of pilgrims, tourists, and other visitors come to the Santuario every year to gather dirt from this hole, many believing it has miraculous healing properties. Sometimes called the “Lourdes of America,” the Santuario is one of the largest sites of Catholic pilgrimage in North America and a monument to the Hispanic and Catholic origins of much of what is modern United States. In this anecdote-rich talk about the Santuario, Prof. Brett Hendrickson will discuss the various political and religious regimes that have ruled New Mexico, examine the meaning of miracles in Hispanic Catholic contexts, and suggest that our nation’s origin stories don’t just start on the East Coast but also belong in the U.S.-Mexico borderlands.

Please register to attend.

title: The Critique Group
description:
Evanston Writers Workshop
We have a new home! We will be meeting at the Art Maker's Outpost 609 South Blvd, Evanston, IL 60202. It is one block west and one block north of the South Blvd L station.

Welcome to the Evanston Writers Workshop Critique Group. One of two foundational programs of EWW, the Critique Group is a group of professional and aspiring writers who help each other polish their writing. Through workshop-style critique, writers have the opportunity to receive direct feedback from attentive readers who understand the craft of writing. For more information, weekly submission links, as well as submission guidelines and requirements, please visit the Evanston Writers Workshop message board here [https://www.meetup.com/evanstonwriters/messages/boards/](https://www.meetup.com/evanstonwriters/messages/boards/) or our website at [www.evannstonwritersworkshop.org](www.evannstonwritersworkshop.org) .

Friendly reminder: please have your submissions uploaded by the Friday (midnight) before the meeting.

title: The Block Online Collection Talk - MARSHA: The Joy and Defiance of Marsha P. Johnson
description:
The Block online collection talk by Llweyn Blossfeld and Isabella Ko will be on Thursday April 2nd from 12:30-1 pm.
They will be introducing us to pieces in the Block's collection that speak to the Women's Center's chosen text for this year, _MARSHA: The Joy and Defiance of Marsha P. Johnson_ by Tourmaline.
This event is one of many marking forty years of education and collaboration at the Women's Center.

title: “Marsha:” Online Collection Talk
description:
Join Block Museum staff for a talk considering an artwork from the Block’s collection that reflects on legacies of living free, queer activism, and the trans experience, in connection to the 2025-2026 Women’s Center book selection, Marsha: The Joy and Defiance of Marsha P. Johnson by Tourmaline.
Our Spring Quarter talk by Llewyn Blossfeld, Curatorial Associate, will focus on Caprice, 55, Chicago, IL, 2015 by Jess T. Dugan and Vanessa Fabre. Other works in the Block's collection will also be referenced.  The program will be facilitated by Isabella Ko, Engagement Coordinator and Educator.
In honor of the Women’s Center’s 40th anniversary and their theme for the academic year,  Legacies of Living Free, the Women’s Center is inviting the Northwestern Community to connect deeply and collectively with a single text, Marsha: The Joy and Defiance of Marsha P. Johnson by Tourmaline.  The Block is proud to partner with the Women’s Center to explore the themes of this shared text, selecting artworks from the museum collection that can broaden discussions.
Llewyn Blossfeld (he/they) is Curatorial Associate at the Block Museum of Art. He is an art historian and writer pursuing a Dual Degree MA in Art History and Arts Administration at the School of the Art Institute, with research interests in transgender history, disability and queer studies, and LGBTQ+ portraiture, as well as the many intersections of art and politics. He has previously worked at the Blanton Museum of Art, the Green Family Art Foundation, and the Museum of Contemporary Art Chicago. At the Block Museum, he continues to support research on artworks entering the collection, especially as this research aids our collecting goals in support of teaching and research at Northwestern University.
Isabella Ko (she/her) aspires to create spaces where people can slow down, get curious, and reflect on the world around them—while in the company of art and others. She wonders about how to center critical pedagogical practices when facilitating art experiences and the role of cultural production within sociopolitical movements. Isabella is the Block Museum’s Engagement Coordinator & Educator and graduated from Northwestern University in 2020 with a major in art history and minors in French and Business Institutions. You might hear her before you see her (talking and laughing loudly with loved ones.)
The Block Museum of Art acknowledges support from the Illinois Arts Council.
Image credits: Jacket design by Jason Booher.  Jacket art based on image of Marsha P. Johnson from Randy Wicker & Marsha P. Johnson Papers via The LGBT Center.  Art Circle: Jess T. Dugan (American, born 1982) and Vanessa Fabre (American, born 1978), Caprice, 55, Chicago, IL, 2015, from the portfolio To Survive on This Shore: Photographs and Interviews with Transgender and Gender Nonconforming Older Adults, 2018, Inkjet print on paper.  Mary and Leigh Block Museum of Art, Northwestern University, The Block Friends of Art Fund purchase.  2019.2.1q-r.

title: Honors Program Speaker: Patrick Hassan
description:
Iran has a long history of foreign interference in its domestic affairs. Arguably, this context of foreign interference has been the primary force shaping how the ruling body of the Islamic Republic of Iran purportedly conceives of itself and its governing principles, namely: as the sole entity capable of upholding Iran's national sovereignty in the face of foreign (i.e., Western) domination. From a realist perspective, this talk explores how the Islamic Republic's projected anti-imperialist stance—something increasingly repeated by some portions of the Western Left—might be best understood and evaluated in light of its demonstrated practical aims, and especially the means it has frequently taken in pursuit of those aims (e.g., severe domestic repression; proxy warfare). By doing so, I aim to shed light on two broader philosophical themes potentially instructive beyond the Iranian case: (a) how and why hawkish or liberal-interventionist strategies can be self-undermining; (b) how and why ideological ‘camp’ thinking and selective solidarity can produce dangerous double standards among otherwise well-meaning anti-imperialists.

Please register to attend.

title: Clothesline Project Art Making Space | Sexual Assault Action Month
location: Foster-Walker Complex, 1927 Orrington Avenue, Evanston, IL
description:
Gather with CSAW to share a message of healing, resilience, and hope for survivors of interpersonal violence. T-shirts and art supplies for decoration will be provided.

The Clothesline Project is an interactive exhibit of messages of resilience, healing, and hope for individuals and communities impacted by interpersonal violence and abuse. These messages are shared on t-shirts, which visually represent the statistics of those impacted by violence.  College campuses, crisis centers, and community organizations throughout the U.S. and around the world have hosted Clothesline Project events since the 1990s to acknowledge the magnitude of the impacts of interpersonal violence.

title: Lee & Grant at Appomattox Court House: A Day of Reckoning
description: In the final days of the American Civil War, two men both trained in the art of war but from vastly different backgrounds would come together to begin the process of ending the conflict and returning the nation to peace.  What would come out of those meetings would not only set the tone for how other armies still in the fight would transition from war to peace but also how the nation would move forward from the bloodiest conflict in American History.

title: Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
The storied city of Djenné, a center of Islamic learning, study, and scholarship since the twelfth century, is the hometown of Bamako-based photographer Hamdia Traoré (b. 1992, Mali). The thirty portraits in Traore’s series Des marabouts de Djenné (Marabouts of Jenne) reflect his intimate connections to the city’s people and deep history. Learned and devout, marabouts teach in Djenné’s over 50 Qur’anic schools, offer spiritual guidance, and treat ailments through their knowledge of the Qur’an.
Made during a time of political and social upheaval in Mali, these portraits reflect enduring cultural resilience. Each image depicts a marabout seated with the tools of his practice—books, Qur’an boards, amulets, and prayer beads—framed by the architecture and atmosphere of Djenné. The consistent format underscores their collective identity, while individual poses and captions highlight personal roles and neighborhoods.
Traoré’s work will be shown alongside mid-20th-century black-and-white portraits of marabouts by Malian photographers Mamadou Cissé, Abdourahmane Sakaly, and Tijani Sitou. Drawn from the Archive of Malian Photography, these earlier images share visual parallels and deepen the historical context. Together, these images illuminate evolving perspectives on spiritual authority, identity, and visual representation in Mali.

Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali” is curated by Kathleen Bickford Berzock, Associate Director of Curatorial Affairs, The Block Museum of Art, Northwestern University, in collaboration with Candace M. Keller, Associate Professor, Art History & Visual Culture, Michigan State University.  We are grateful to the Archive of Malian Photography (https://amp.matrix.msu.edu/), which provided digital scans of archival works. At The Block, the exhibition is supported by the Kadin/Spiegel Family Endowed Fund and a grant from the Illinois Arts Council.

title: Teresa Montoya’s Tó Łitso (Yellow Water): Ten Years after the Gold King Mine Spill
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
On August 5, 2015, the rupture of the abandoned Gold King Mine near Silverton, Colorado, released more than three million gallons of toxic wastewater into the Animas River, turning its waters a shocking shade of yellow. In the following year, artist and anthropologist Teresa Montoya (Diné, born 1984) embarked on a road trip from Silverton to Shiprock, New Mexico, retracing the path of the contaminated water and documenting its ongoing cultural, spiritual, and material effects on the Navajo Nation and other Indigenous communities downstream.
Marking the ten-year anniversary of the disaster, The Block Museum of Art partners with Montoya to revisit this journey. Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) explores the enduring consequences of the Gold King Mine spill through photography, sound recordings, water samples, and cartographic data.
Combining documentary photographs with scientific data and poetic reflection, Tó Łitso invites viewers to consider water not only as a life-sustaining resource but also as a conduit for histories, stories, and harm. In doing so, the exhibition challenges extractive frameworks of land use, centering Indigenous knowledge and resilience. Through Montoya’s interdisciplinary practice, Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) offers a powerful meditation on environmental and cultural justice in the Southwest and beyond.
"The 2015 spill had discharged more than three million gallons of acidic mine waste fluid into Cement Creek before joining the Animas River and eventually into the San Juan River, which flows directly across the Navajo Nation. Through my photographic journey, I make explicit the enduring presence of toxicity across multiple landscapes and territories. Sometimes it appears beautiful, other times haunting. The images highlight the relationships that various communities sustain through water/tó despite the occurrence of repeated and enduring contamination from upstream locales. The Gold King Mine Spill shows us this, even in that, which cannot be readily seen." – Teresa Montoya

title: Café Conversation Matin
description:
Alliance Française du North Shore
Join fellow Francophones for relaxed, mid-morning conversations in French on first Mondays, facilitated by one or more of our native or fluent AFduNS leaders.

All fluency levels are welcome.

For further info, visit [afnorthshore.org](https://afnorthshore.org) or contact cafe-matin@AFnorthshore.org.

**Membership in the AFduNS**
While no membership is required for newcomers, we welcome new members whose membership will support the activities of the AFduNS, a nonprofit 501(c)(3) organization. For further info, visit our [website](https://afnorthshore.org/events) or [click here to contact us](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLScRxM03DpryO_lZlGWK-aDOCiFHFbivJimTScMOQmWk7KgOJQ/viewform?pli=1). If you wish to join the AFduNS, you may sign up [online](https://afnorthshore.org/join). Annual dues are $50.00.

title: Downtown Evanston Earth Day CleanUp
description:
Join Downtown Evanston and The Woman's Club of Evanston on Sunday, April 26, from noon to 2 p.m. for Downtown Evanston's annual Earth Day Clean-Up event aimed at fostering community engagement and environmental stewardship. Individuals, families, office groups, students are all encouraged to lend a hand in making our downtown business district a cleaner, greener place to live, work and visit!

Participants will gather at Fountain Square to get outfitted with gloves, bags and maps before heading out to pick-up litter and debris. When finished, participants will bring the trash bags back to Fountain Square for disposal.

title: Amanda Majeski Vocal Master Class
location: Patrick G. and Shirley W. Ryan Center for the Musical Arts, 70 Arts Circle, Evanston, IL
description:
Part of the Tichio-Finnie Vocal Master Class Series
Lurdes Zapata and Maya Behiri, soprano; Isabel Yang, mezzo-soprano; Leonardo Mangalindan, tenor; Alan Darling, piano

The Financial Times remarked that soprano Amanda Majeski’s (‘06) “well-rounded soprano... is so warm and glorious, the singing so outstanding, that she leaves no emotions unstirred.” A celebrated interpreter of Mozart, Strauss, Wagner, and Handel, Majeski is acclaimed for her portrayal of the title role in Janáček’s Káťa Kabanová, which she debuted at the Royal Opera House, Covent Garden in 2019. Prior to that performance, she made her Metropolitan Opera debut as Countess Almaviva in a 2014 production of Le nozze di Figaro and reprised the role for her mainstage debut at the Lyric Opera of Chicago on just a few hours’ notice. Her 2025-26 season began with her debut in the title role of Shostakovich’s Lady Macbeth of Mtsensk at the BBC Proms and includes a return to Oper Frankfurt as Marta in The Passenger, as well as appearances as the Marschallin in Der Rosenkavalier with New Orleans Opera and engagements with the Rundfunk-Sinfonieorchester Berlin for Szymanowski’s Stabat Mater and Mahler’s Fourth Symphony. In this master class, she coaches accomplished Bienen School voice and opera students.

title: Strategic Storytelling: Nonfiction & Narrative | Sexual Assault Action Month
location: Women's Center, 2000 Sheridan Rd., Evanston, IL
description:
For many survivors of interpersonal violence, telling their story can be a crucial part of the healing journey. Being honest and vulnerable in nonfiction writing can be a powerful experience, but it can present its own set of challenges, especially if that writing is shared with the world. This workshop will explore the many avenues that survivors may choose to explore their experiences through narrative nonfiction writing, offer prompts for reflection and self-compassion when sharing stories of harm, discuss how survivors can maintain their safety and well-being when sharing their stories, and hold space for free writing.
4/7 7:00pm Women’s Center  | Food provided | RSVP Required

title: Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
The storied city of Djenné, a center of Islamic learning, study, and scholarship since the twelfth century, is the hometown of Bamako-based photographer Hamdia Traoré (b. 1992, Mali). The thirty portraits in Traore’s series Des marabouts de Djenné (Marabouts of Jenne) reflect his intimate connections to the city’s people and deep history. Learned and devout, marabouts teach in Djenné’s over 50 Qur’anic schools, offer spiritual guidance, and treat ailments through their knowledge of the Qur’an.
Made during a time of political and social upheaval in Mali, these portraits reflect enduring cultural resilience. Each image depicts a marabout seated with the tools of his practice—books, Qur’an boards, amulets, and prayer beads—framed by the architecture and atmosphere of Djenné. The consistent format underscores their collective identity, while individual poses and captions highlight personal roles and neighborhoods.
Traoré’s work will be shown alongside mid-20th-century black-and-white portraits of marabouts by Malian photographers Mamadou Cissé, Abdourahmane Sakaly, and Tijani Sitou. Drawn from the Archive of Malian Photography, these earlier images share visual parallels and deepen the historical context. Together, these images illuminate evolving perspectives on spiritual authority, identity, and visual representation in Mali.

Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali” is curated by Kathleen Bickford Berzock, Associate Director of Curatorial Affairs, The Block Museum of Art, Northwestern University, in collaboration with Candace M. Keller, Associate Professor, Art History & Visual Culture, Michigan State University.  We are grateful to the Archive of Malian Photography (https://amp.matrix.msu.edu/), which provided digital scans of archival works. At The Block, the exhibition is supported by the Kadin/Spiegel Family Endowed Fund and a grant from the Illinois Arts Council.

title: Teresa Montoya’s Tó Łitso (Yellow Water): Ten Years after the Gold King Mine Spill
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
On August 5, 2015, the rupture of the abandoned Gold King Mine near Silverton, Colorado, released more than three million gallons of toxic wastewater into the Animas River, turning its waters a shocking shade of yellow. In the following year, artist and anthropologist Teresa Montoya (Diné, born 1984) embarked on a road trip from Silverton to Shiprock, New Mexico, retracing the path of the contaminated water and documenting its ongoing cultural, spiritual, and material effects on the Navajo Nation and other Indigenous communities downstream.
Marking the ten-year anniversary of the disaster, The Block Museum of Art partners with Montoya to revisit this journey. Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) explores the enduring consequences of the Gold King Mine spill through photography, sound recordings, water samples, and cartographic data.
Combining documentary photographs with scientific data and poetic reflection, Tó Łitso invites viewers to consider water not only as a life-sustaining resource but also as a conduit for histories, stories, and harm. In doing so, the exhibition challenges extractive frameworks of land use, centering Indigenous knowledge and resilience. Through Montoya’s interdisciplinary practice, Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) offers a powerful meditation on environmental and cultural justice in the Southwest and beyond.
"The 2015 spill had discharged more than three million gallons of acidic mine waste fluid into Cement Creek before joining the Animas River and eventually into the San Juan River, which flows directly across the Navajo Nation. Through my photographic journey, I make explicit the enduring presence of toxicity across multiple landscapes and territories. Sometimes it appears beautiful, other times haunting. The images highlight the relationships that various communities sustain through water/tó despite the occurrence of repeated and enduring contamination from upstream locales. The Gold King Mine Spill shows us this, even in that, which cannot be readily seen." – Teresa Montoya

title: Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
The storied city of Djenné, a center of Islamic learning, study, and scholarship since the twelfth century, is the hometown of Bamako-based photographer Hamdia Traoré (b. 1992, Mali). The thirty portraits in Traore’s series Des marabouts de Djenné (Marabouts of Jenne) reflect his intimate connections to the city’s people and deep history. Learned and devout, marabouts teach in Djenné’s over 50 Qur’anic schools, offer spiritual guidance, and treat ailments through their knowledge of the Qur’an.
Made during a time of political and social upheaval in Mali, these portraits reflect enduring cultural resilience. Each image depicts a marabout seated with the tools of his practice—books, Qur’an boards, amulets, and prayer beads—framed by the architecture and atmosphere of Djenné. The consistent format underscores their collective identity, while individual poses and captions highlight personal roles and neighborhoods.
Traoré’s work will be shown alongside mid-20th-century black-and-white portraits of marabouts by Malian photographers Mamadou Cissé, Abdourahmane Sakaly, and Tijani Sitou. Drawn from the Archive of Malian Photography, these earlier images share visual parallels and deepen the historical context. Together, these images illuminate evolving perspectives on spiritual authority, identity, and visual representation in Mali.

Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali” is curated by Kathleen Bickford Berzock, Associate Director of Curatorial Affairs, The Block Museum of Art, Northwestern University, in collaboration with Candace M. Keller, Associate Professor, Art History & Visual Culture, Michigan State University.  We are grateful to the Archive of Malian Photography (https://amp.matrix.msu.edu/), which provided digital scans of archival works. At The Block, the exhibition is supported by the Kadin/Spiegel Family Endowed Fund and a grant from the Illinois Arts Council.

title: Teresa Montoya’s Tó Łitso (Yellow Water): Ten Years after the Gold King Mine Spill
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
On August 5, 2015, the rupture of the abandoned Gold King Mine near Silverton, Colorado, released more than three million gallons of toxic wastewater into the Animas River, turning its waters a shocking shade of yellow. In the following year, artist and anthropologist Teresa Montoya (Diné, born 1984) embarked on a road trip from Silverton to Shiprock, New Mexico, retracing the path of the contaminated water and documenting its ongoing cultural, spiritual, and material effects on the Navajo Nation and other Indigenous communities downstream.
Marking the ten-year anniversary of the disaster, The Block Museum of Art partners with Montoya to revisit this journey. Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) explores the enduring consequences of the Gold King Mine spill through photography, sound recordings, water samples, and cartographic data.
Combining documentary photographs with scientific data and poetic reflection, Tó Łitso invites viewers to consider water not only as a life-sustaining resource but also as a conduit for histories, stories, and harm. In doing so, the exhibition challenges extractive frameworks of land use, centering Indigenous knowledge and resilience. Through Montoya’s interdisciplinary practice, Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) offers a powerful meditation on environmental and cultural justice in the Southwest and beyond.
"The 2015 spill had discharged more than three million gallons of acidic mine waste fluid into Cement Creek before joining the Animas River and eventually into the San Juan River, which flows directly across the Navajo Nation. Through my photographic journey, I make explicit the enduring presence of toxicity across multiple landscapes and territories. Sometimes it appears beautiful, other times haunting. The images highlight the relationships that various communities sustain through water/tó despite the occurrence of repeated and enduring contamination from upstream locales. The Gold King Mine Spill shows us this, even in that, which cannot be readily seen." – Teresa Montoya

title: Opening Reception: Community Art Show
location: Norris University Center, 1999 Campus Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
Join us for the opening reception of our annual community show which brings together artists from Northwestern, Evanston and greater Chicagoland communities to display their works in a group show.
Art has been used throughout human existence as a unifying force and a wordless language to represent our curiosities, communities, and aspirations. However, artistic spaces and practices have not always been inclusive or accessible to everyone. For this community show, we invited artists to consider how their perspectives could be integrated to manipulate and redefine art in spaces in order to reclaim personal identities, art practices, and cultures that have complex histories. Pieces were selected by how well they explored how creativity could be exercised to navigate the world and take ownership of reality. Artists were encouraged to consider stepping outside of the traditions of academic art to notice what they encounter in day-to-day life that could be considered art.

title: Garrick Ohlsson, piano
location: Patrick G. and Shirley W. Ryan Center for the Musical Arts, 70 Arts Circle, Evanston, IL
description:
Part of the Skyline Piano Artist Series
“Impeccable clarity and architecture” —International Piano
Since triumphing as the first and only American winner of the 1970 Chopin International Piano Competition, Garrick Ohlsson has established himself worldwide as a musician of magisterial interpretive and technical prowess. While he is an authority on Chopin’s works, Ohlsson performs a vast repertoire often including the music of Mozart, Beethoven, Schubert, and the Romanticists. As an avid chamber musician, Ohlsson has collaborated with the Cleveland, Emerson, Tokyo, and Takacs string quartets as well as legendary vocalists such as Magda Olivero and Jessye Norman. Ohlsson’s recordings are highly awarded, including Beethoven Sonatas, Vol. 3, which won the 2008 Grammy for Best Solo Recording. Ohlsson has been featured on live performance recordings with the Melbourne and Sydney Symphonies and the Czech Philharmonic. Throughout his career, Ohlsson has been awarded such prizes as the Avery Fisher Prize, the University Musical Society Distinguished Artist Award, Northwestern University’s Jean Gimbel Lane Prize in Piano Performance, and the Gloria Artis Gold Medal for cultural merit from the Polish Deputy Culture Minister.
Ludwig van Beethoven, Theme and Variations in F Major, Op. 34
Sergei Prokofiev, Sonata No. 8, Op. 84
Frédéric Chopin, Nocturne in F Major, Op. 15, No. 1
Frédéric Chopin, Piano Sonata No. 3 in B Minor, Op. 58

title: Raphaella Smits, guitar
location: Patrick G. and Shirley W. Ryan Center for the Musical Arts, 70 Arts Circle, Evanston, IL
description:
Part of the Segovia Classical Guitar Series
"...one of the most delicate and most cultivated performers of our time."
—Diapason
A renowned guitarist and pedagogue, Raphaella Smits plays worldwide on eight-string guitars and historical instruments. Alongside her international touring career, she has recorded 25 albums to great acclaim. Smits is recognized as a gifted teacher of both guitar and chamber music, frequently serving on the juries of prestigious international music competitions. She taught at the Lemmens Institute in Belgium for 36 years and continues to give master classes across Europe, the Americas, and Japan. In 1986, Smits was the first woman to win the first prize of the Francisco Tárrega International Guitar Competition in Spain, earning praise from chairmen Andrés Segovia and Narciso Yepes. Smits was recently awarded at the Festival Guitarras del Mundo in Argentina for her unique contributions to guitar music and culture.
Mauro Giuliani, Grande ouverture, Op. 61
Fernando Sor, Introduction et variations sur l’air “Malbroug,” Op. 28
Fernando Sor, Fantaisie pour la guitare dédiée à son ami Ignace Pleyel, Op. 7
Anton Diabelli, Sonatina No. 3, Op. 168
Fernando Sor, Etude No. 11, Op. 6
Johann Kaspar Mertz, Le romantique (Grande fantaisie)
Segovia Classical Guitar Series new subscriptions and single tickets are available now.

title: Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
The storied city of Djenné, a center of Islamic learning, study, and scholarship since the twelfth century, is the hometown of Bamako-based photographer Hamdia Traoré (b. 1992, Mali). The thirty portraits in Traore’s series Des marabouts de Djenné (Marabouts of Jenne) reflect his intimate connections to the city’s people and deep history. Learned and devout, marabouts teach in Djenné’s over 50 Qur’anic schools, offer spiritual guidance, and treat ailments through their knowledge of the Qur’an.
Made during a time of political and social upheaval in Mali, these portraits reflect enduring cultural resilience. Each image depicts a marabout seated with the tools of his practice—books, Qur’an boards, amulets, and prayer beads—framed by the architecture and atmosphere of Djenné. The consistent format underscores their collective identity, while individual poses and captions highlight personal roles and neighborhoods.
Traoré’s work will be shown alongside mid-20th-century black-and-white portraits of marabouts by Malian photographers Mamadou Cissé, Abdourahmane Sakaly, and Tijani Sitou. Drawn from the Archive of Malian Photography, these earlier images share visual parallels and deepen the historical context. Together, these images illuminate evolving perspectives on spiritual authority, identity, and visual representation in Mali.

Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali” is curated by Kathleen Bickford Berzock, Associate Director of Curatorial Affairs, The Block Museum of Art, Northwestern University, in collaboration with Candace M. Keller, Associate Professor, Art History & Visual Culture, Michigan State University.  We are grateful to the Archive of Malian Photography (https://amp.matrix.msu.edu/), which provided digital scans of archival works. At The Block, the exhibition is supported by the Kadin/Spiegel Family Endowed Fund and a grant from the Illinois Arts Council.

title: Teresa Montoya’s Tó Łitso (Yellow Water): Ten Years after the Gold King Mine Spill
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
On August 5, 2015, the rupture of the abandoned Gold King Mine near Silverton, Colorado, released more than three million gallons of toxic wastewater into the Animas River, turning its waters a shocking shade of yellow. In the following year, artist and anthropologist Teresa Montoya (Diné, born 1984) embarked on a road trip from Silverton to Shiprock, New Mexico, retracing the path of the contaminated water and documenting its ongoing cultural, spiritual, and material effects on the Navajo Nation and other Indigenous communities downstream.
Marking the ten-year anniversary of the disaster, The Block Museum of Art partners with Montoya to revisit this journey. Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) explores the enduring consequences of the Gold King Mine spill through photography, sound recordings, water samples, and cartographic data.
Combining documentary photographs with scientific data and poetic reflection, Tó Łitso invites viewers to consider water not only as a life-sustaining resource but also as a conduit for histories, stories, and harm. In doing so, the exhibition challenges extractive frameworks of land use, centering Indigenous knowledge and resilience. Through Montoya’s interdisciplinary practice, Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) offers a powerful meditation on environmental and cultural justice in the Southwest and beyond.
"The 2015 spill had discharged more than three million gallons of acidic mine waste fluid into Cement Creek before joining the Animas River and eventually into the San Juan River, which flows directly across the Navajo Nation. Through my photographic journey, I make explicit the enduring presence of toxicity across multiple landscapes and territories. Sometimes it appears beautiful, other times haunting. The images highlight the relationships that various communities sustain through water/tó despite the occurrence of repeated and enduring contamination from upstream locales. The Gold King Mine Spill shows us this, even in that, which cannot be readily seen." – Teresa Montoya

title: Gardening for the Seasons
description: Set within the contemporary design of Millennium Park and against the urban backdrop of downtown Chicago, Lurie Garden has earned a reputation as a premier example of naturalistic garden design. Using the garden as our inspiration and drawing from the masterful vision of its perennial plant designer, Piet Oudolf, this talk presented by Kathryn Deery will explore a fundamental shift in our approach to gardening. We will move our focus from routine maintenance to thoughtful management, delve into the art of designing with both ornamental and native plants, and share advice for cutting back your garden to support wildlife and insects. With a few simple adjustments to the way we garden, a beautifully designed space that considers sustainability and ecology is within everyone’s reach.Kathryn serves as the director of horticulture for Lurie Garden and Millennium Park. Driven by a passion for the vital role public gardens play in communities, she works to maintain the design integrity of Lurie Garden while facilitating its continuous evolution. She also ensures that Millennium Park maintains a high standard of horticulture by prioritizing both its beauty and its ecological function.This event will be recorded and will be accessible on our YouTube events channel the following week.

title: Screenplay Critique Group
description:
Evanston Writers Workshop
Welcome to the Evanston Writers Workshop’s **Screenplay Critique Group**. We're a group of professional and aspiring writers who help each other polish their writing through workshop style critique. Writers have the opportunity to receive and give direct, constructive, and actionable feedback from attentive readers and writers who understand the craft of screenwriting.

## Details

**\- This group is for Screenwriters writers\, aged 18\+ at** **INTERMEDIATE to ADVANCED levels only**.

* We will meet monthly on **the second Tuesday of the month, from 7:00-9:00pm central time on ZOOM**.
\- We will critique up to 3 submissions \(up to 10 pages in Standard Screenplay Format\) per meeting\.
\- You do not need to be a member to attend or critique\, however **you need to be a member in order to submit and have your own work critiqued**.
\- This is a GIVE & TAKE group\. In exchange for giving thoughtful critique on others’ work\, you get thoughtful critique for your own\.
\- You must RSVP to each Screenplay Critique Group session in order to receive the submissions we will be critiquing\.
\- If you have a submission to be critiqued:

1. To submit, please [CLICK HERE](https://form.jotform.com/251125391424147) to upload your submissions or type
https://form.jotform.com/251125391424147 into your browser **by midnight the Friday before critique meeting**.
2. Put "**Screenplay Critique Group Submission” in the subject header.** (We have many different critique groups and want to make sure it’s directed to the correct folder.)
3. Include **your email address** and **short** bio (this group is for intermediate and advanced screenplay writers) inside your document so participants know where to send their notes.

EWW is a not-for-profit 501 (C) 3 organization founded in 2007. For more information about Evanston Writers Workshop, and membership, visit [evanstonwritersworkshop.org.](http://evanstonwritersworkshop.org./) We have critique groups for all writing levels and genres including for fiction, memoir/non-fiction, and poetry that meet in person in Evanston, IL and also online.
*Friendly reminder: please send your submissions by the Friday (midnight) before the meeting.*

title: Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
The storied city of Djenné, a center of Islamic learning, study, and scholarship since the twelfth century, is the hometown of Bamako-based photographer Hamdia Traoré (b. 1992, Mali). The thirty portraits in Traore’s series Des marabouts de Djenné (Marabouts of Jenne) reflect his intimate connections to the city’s people and deep history. Learned and devout, marabouts teach in Djenné’s over 50 Qur’anic schools, offer spiritual guidance, and treat ailments through their knowledge of the Qur’an.
Made during a time of political and social upheaval in Mali, these portraits reflect enduring cultural resilience. Each image depicts a marabout seated with the tools of his practice—books, Qur’an boards, amulets, and prayer beads—framed by the architecture and atmosphere of Djenné. The consistent format underscores their collective identity, while individual poses and captions highlight personal roles and neighborhoods.
Traoré’s work will be shown alongside mid-20th-century black-and-white portraits of marabouts by Malian photographers Mamadou Cissé, Abdourahmane Sakaly, and Tijani Sitou. Drawn from the Archive of Malian Photography, these earlier images share visual parallels and deepen the historical context. Together, these images illuminate evolving perspectives on spiritual authority, identity, and visual representation in Mali.

Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali” is curated by Kathleen Bickford Berzock, Associate Director of Curatorial Affairs, The Block Museum of Art, Northwestern University, in collaboration with Candace M. Keller, Associate Professor, Art History & Visual Culture, Michigan State University.  We are grateful to the Archive of Malian Photography (https://amp.matrix.msu.edu/), which provided digital scans of archival works. At The Block, the exhibition is supported by the Kadin/Spiegel Family Endowed Fund and a grant from the Illinois Arts Council.

title: Teresa Montoya’s Tó Łitso (Yellow Water): Ten Years after the Gold King Mine Spill
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
On August 5, 2015, the rupture of the abandoned Gold King Mine near Silverton, Colorado, released more than three million gallons of toxic wastewater into the Animas River, turning its waters a shocking shade of yellow. In the following year, artist and anthropologist Teresa Montoya (Diné, born 1984) embarked on a road trip from Silverton to Shiprock, New Mexico, retracing the path of the contaminated water and documenting its ongoing cultural, spiritual, and material effects on the Navajo Nation and other Indigenous communities downstream.
Marking the ten-year anniversary of the disaster, The Block Museum of Art partners with Montoya to revisit this journey. Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) explores the enduring consequences of the Gold King Mine spill through photography, sound recordings, water samples, and cartographic data.
Combining documentary photographs with scientific data and poetic reflection, Tó Łitso invites viewers to consider water not only as a life-sustaining resource but also as a conduit for histories, stories, and harm. In doing so, the exhibition challenges extractive frameworks of land use, centering Indigenous knowledge and resilience. Through Montoya’s interdisciplinary practice, Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) offers a powerful meditation on environmental and cultural justice in the Southwest and beyond.
"The 2015 spill had discharged more than three million gallons of acidic mine waste fluid into Cement Creek before joining the Animas River and eventually into the San Juan River, which flows directly across the Navajo Nation. Through my photographic journey, I make explicit the enduring presence of toxicity across multiple landscapes and territories. Sometimes it appears beautiful, other times haunting. The images highlight the relationships that various communities sustain through water/tó despite the occurrence of repeated and enduring contamination from upstream locales. The Gold King Mine Spill shows us this, even in that, which cannot be readily seen." – Teresa Montoya

title: Sorting It Out: Understanding Winnetka's New Waste and Recycling Tools
description: Join us for a waste-related presentation highlighting programming changes in Winnetka, including the introduction of the Recycle Coach app, the Hefty ReNew curbside collection for hard-to-recycle soft plastics, and the sunsetting of the polystyrene drop-off collection at Winnetka Public Works. Christina Seibert, Executive Director of the Solid Waste Agency of Northern Cook County, and Elyse Steiner, Sustainability Coordinator for the Village of Winnetka, will co-present with Liz Kunkle of Go Green Winnetka and Recycle Coach. This event is sponsored by Go Green Winnetka.

title: The History of Billy Joel
description: Though Billy Joel has not released a new rock album in over 27 years, he remains one of the world’s most popular live performers.  Using performance and interview video clips we’ll trace the arc of Billy’s career from his humble beginnings in Hicksville, NY to his slow rise to fame and his long string of hits including “Just The Way You Are”, ”It’s Still Rock N Roll To Me”, ”Uptown Girl” and “River Of Dreams” to his love life including his marriage to supermodel Christie Brinkley and finally to his current status as one of the world’s most successful touring acts.  “If you’re in the mood for a melody…the Piano Man will make you feel alright.”

title: Between the Lines: The Funeral Ladies of Ellerie County by Claire Swinarski
location: 6140 Lincoln Avenue, Morton Grove, IL, 60053, US
description: Between the Lines is now held on two different days, either in person or on Zoom. Titles are always available on Hoopla, as well as other library ebook platforms. New members are warmly welcome! Book Synopsis: Armed with a Crock-Pot and a pile of recipes, a grandmother, her granddaughter, and a mysterious young man work to bring a community together in this uplifting novel for readers of The Chicken Sisters.Esther Larson has been cooking for funerals in the Northwoods of Wisconsin for seventy years. Known locally as the “funeral ladies,” she and her cohort have worked hard to keep the mourners of Ellerie County fed—it is her firm belief that there is very little a warm casserole and a piece of cherry pie can’t fix. But, after falling for an internet scam that puts her home at risk, the proud Larson family matriarch is the one in need of help these days.Iris, Esther’s whip-smart Gen Z granddaughter, would do anything for her family and her community. As she watches her friends and family move out of their lakeside town onto bigger and better things, Iris wonders why she feels so left behind in the place she is desperate to make her home. But when Cooper Welsh shows up, she finally starts to feel like she’s found the missing piece of her puzzle.Cooper is dealing with becoming a legal guardian to his younger half-sister after his beloved stepmother dies. While their celebrity-chef father is focused on his booming career and top-ranked television show, Cooper is still hurting from a public tragedy he witnessed last year as a paramedic and finding it hard to cope. With Iris in the gorgeous Ellerie County, though, he hopes he might finally find the home he’s been looking for.It doesn’t seem like a community cookbook could possibly solve their problems, especially one where casseroles have their own section and cream of chicken soup mix is the most frequently used ingredient. But when you mix the can-do spirit of Midwestern grandmothers with the stubborn hope of a boy raised by food plus a dash of long-awaited forgiveness—things might just turn out okay.

Join us for the annual Arts and Crafts fair at Norris University Center. Each year, the Arts and Crafts fair draws hundreds of campus and Evanston community members to support Northwestern community artists. Unique art pieces will be available for purchase at a variety of price points, allowing for the perfect decoration or gift. Featured artists include ceramics, jewelry, paintings, photography, fine art prints, crochet, stained glass, sculptures, and more.

title: Tourmaline: Legacies of Living Free. The 40th Anniversary Keynote Address for the Women's Center
description:
Best selling author and activist Tourmaline will speak via Webinar about her work on _Marsha: The Joy and Defiance of Marsha P. Johnson_ and its connections to the theme we are using to describe forty years of community, education, and advocacy at the Women's Center: Legacies of Living Free.
We will have time to enjoy Tourmaline in conversation with our own beloved Marquis Bey, author of Black Trans Feminisms and take questions from our audience.
This event will be held via Zoom Webinar. It is free and open to all. Please feel free to share with you networks and, if you are in the Evanston or Chicago area, see our website for information about acquiring a free copy of the book, _Marsha: The Joy and Defiance of Marsha P. Johnson_.

title: Clean Living: Lessons from a Chemical War: A talk by Dr. Thuy Tu
location: Crowe Hall, 1860 Campus Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
For decades, the multi-billion dollar health and wellness industry has touted the virtues of clean living. This lecture asks: What do we mean by clean? How did those ideas shift in the aftermath of the Vietnam War and the U.S. military’s widespread use of chemical weapons? What lessons about living clean can we gather from those who have survived mass contamination?
Thuy Linh Nguyen Tu is Professor of American Studies in the Department of Social and Analysis at NYU. She is the author or editor of five books, most recently, Experiments in Skin: Race and Beauty in the Shadows of Vietnam (Duke UP, 2021), which was honored with multiple awards, including the R. R. Hawkins Award and a Victor Turner Prize. She was the former faculty director of the NYU Prison Education Program's Research Lab and recently the co-leader of the Mellon Sawyer Seminar on "Transpacific Thought and the Problem of Asia."

title: Illinois Libraries Present - Cristina Henríquez:  Voice, Culture, and Human Connection
description: Join critically acclaimed, bestselling author, Cristina Henríquez, for a compelling conversation about her work. Henríquez’s newest book, The Great Divide, is a moving exploration of the people who lived, loved, and labored during the construction of the Panama Canal. Named a New York Times’ Editors’ Choice selection and TIME Magazine “100 Must-Read Books of 2024,” The Great Divide explores history and adversity in a place very special to her – her father’s homeland of Panama.Henríquez has also authored The Book of Unknown Americans, The World in Half, and Come Together, Fall Apart, all to significant acclaim. Her fiction has appeared in The New Yorker, The Atlantic, The New York Times, and The Best American Short Stories 2018, and she is a recipient of the 21st Century Award given by The Chicago Public Library Foundation. She is a graduate of Northwestern University and the Iowa Writer’s Workshop, and lives in the Chicago suburbs with her family.Carmen Álvarez will join Henríquez as moderator. Álvarez is an advocate for libraries and Latinx representation in the publishing industry. Her work has appeared in Glamour, Elle, and Vogue and she has a social media presence surpassing 180k followers across platforms.Illinois Libraries Present (ILP) is a Member based initiative of the Illinois Library Association and a statewide collaboration among public libraries offering premier events. Thank you to our state partners for the continuous support.Partial funding for this season is supported by a grant, awarded by the Illinois State Library, a Department of the Secretary of State, using funds provided by the U.S. Institute of Museum and Library Services, under the provisions of the Library Services and Technology Act (LSTA).ASL Interpreting Services are made possible by the support of Illinois Heartland Library System (IHLS) and Reaching Across Illinois Library System (RAILS).

Named a New York Times Editors’ Choice selection and TIME Magazine “100 Must-Read Books of 2024,” The Great Divide explores history and adversity in a place very special to her–her father’s homeland of Panama. This program will not be shown in person at the library. Register Here!This is an online event, made possible by Illinois Libraries Present, a statewide collaboration between public libraries, including Lincolnwood Public Library.

title: Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
The storied city of Djenné, a center of Islamic learning, study, and scholarship since the twelfth century, is the hometown of Bamako-based photographer Hamdia Traoré (b. 1992, Mali). The thirty portraits in Traore’s series Des marabouts de Djenné (Marabouts of Jenne) reflect his intimate connections to the city’s people and deep history. Learned and devout, marabouts teach in Djenné’s over 50 Qur’anic schools, offer spiritual guidance, and treat ailments through their knowledge of the Qur’an.
Made during a time of political and social upheaval in Mali, these portraits reflect enduring cultural resilience. Each image depicts a marabout seated with the tools of his practice—books, Qur’an boards, amulets, and prayer beads—framed by the architecture and atmosphere of Djenné. The consistent format underscores their collective identity, while individual poses and captions highlight personal roles and neighborhoods.
Traoré’s work will be shown alongside mid-20th-century black-and-white portraits of marabouts by Malian photographers Mamadou Cissé, Abdourahmane Sakaly, and Tijani Sitou. Drawn from the Archive of Malian Photography, these earlier images share visual parallels and deepen the historical context. Together, these images illuminate evolving perspectives on spiritual authority, identity, and visual representation in Mali.

Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali” is curated by Kathleen Bickford Berzock, Associate Director of Curatorial Affairs, The Block Museum of Art, Northwestern University, in collaboration with Candace M. Keller, Associate Professor, Art History & Visual Culture, Michigan State University.  We are grateful to the Archive of Malian Photography (https://amp.matrix.msu.edu/), which provided digital scans of archival works. At The Block, the exhibition is supported by the Kadin/Spiegel Family Endowed Fund and a grant from the Illinois Arts Council.

title: Teresa Montoya’s Tó Łitso (Yellow Water): Ten Years after the Gold King Mine Spill
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
On August 5, 2015, the rupture of the abandoned Gold King Mine near Silverton, Colorado, released more than three million gallons of toxic wastewater into the Animas River, turning its waters a shocking shade of yellow. In the following year, artist and anthropologist Teresa Montoya (Diné, born 1984) embarked on a road trip from Silverton to Shiprock, New Mexico, retracing the path of the contaminated water and documenting its ongoing cultural, spiritual, and material effects on the Navajo Nation and other Indigenous communities downstream.
Marking the ten-year anniversary of the disaster, The Block Museum of Art partners with Montoya to revisit this journey. Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) explores the enduring consequences of the Gold King Mine spill through photography, sound recordings, water samples, and cartographic data.
Combining documentary photographs with scientific data and poetic reflection, Tó Łitso invites viewers to consider water not only as a life-sustaining resource but also as a conduit for histories, stories, and harm. In doing so, the exhibition challenges extractive frameworks of land use, centering Indigenous knowledge and resilience. Through Montoya’s interdisciplinary practice, Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) offers a powerful meditation on environmental and cultural justice in the Southwest and beyond.
"The 2015 spill had discharged more than three million gallons of acidic mine waste fluid into Cement Creek before joining the Animas River and eventually into the San Juan River, which flows directly across the Navajo Nation. Through my photographic journey, I make explicit the enduring presence of toxicity across multiple landscapes and territories. Sometimes it appears beautiful, other times haunting. The images highlight the relationships that various communities sustain through water/tó despite the occurrence of repeated and enduring contamination from upstream locales. The Gold King Mine Spill shows us this, even in that, which cannot be readily seen." – Teresa Montoya

title: Friday Film
description: Downton Abbey: The Grand Finale It’s time to say goodbye. This is the third movie in the Downton Abbey franchise, which continues the popular TV series. It’s 1930 and the future of Downton Abbey is now in Mary’s hands. However, the scandal of Mary’s pending divorce results in social disgrace, and there are those who think a woman in her position cannot be head of the prominent Crawley family. Think again. Times are changing. You’ll love the beautiful scenery, sets, costumes, and characters who are like old friends. All the storylines come full circle to a very satisfying conclusion.Starring Hugh Bonneville, Jim Carter, Michelle Dockery, Paul Giamatti, Elizabeth McGovern, and Penelope Wilton.2025 : 124 minutes : PG for smoking, suggestive material and some thematic elements : DramaFree tickets will be given to those who attend.Watch a trailer for Downton Abbey: The Grand Finale.Find Downton Abbey: The Grand Finale in the library catalog.

title: Karen Kan-Walsh and Julian Kwok, piano
location: Patrick G. and Shirley W. Ryan Center for the Musical Arts, 70 Arts Circle, Evanston, IL
description:
Coordinator of keyboard skills and non-major piano at the Bienen School, Karen Kan-Walsh has given performances in Russia and China, as well as throughout the Chicago and Ann Arbor areas. An active member of the Illinois State Music Teachers Association, she also served as a reviewer for Clavier magazine. Guest collaborative pianist Julian Kwok (‘03 DMus) maintains a private voice studio in New York and holds certificates in vocal and clarinet performance as well as diplomas in piano performance from the Royal Schools of Music, London. Their program showcases music for two pianos as well as Bach’s Concerto for Two Pianos in C Minor, featuring an ensemble conducted by Yelisey (Eli) Zelman.
Sergei Rachmaninoff, Suite No. 2 for Two Pianos, Op. 17
Manuel Infante, Danses andalouses
J. S. Bach, Concerto for Two Pianos in C Minor, BWV 1060

description:
The Chicago Drum Circle Meetup Group
Dinner as a group at 6:00pm. (not included in price) A fantastic chance to break bread and connect to your drum community. After that, drum lesson and 2, 30 minute drum circles.

Double Clutch Brewery
2121 Ashland Ave, Evanston, IL 60201

followed by an hour long drum lesson. You'll learn
some drum technique and some basic rhythms.
Then make your own beats on the drum, or try

title: Chicago Language Symposium 2026
description:
2026 Chicago Language Symposium
Surviving in a Time of Change: Embracing Challenges as Opportunities
Saturday, April 18th 2026

Online via Zoom; All times Central

At a time of anticipated and unanticipated change, what steps can we take to meet students where they are while strengthening and sustaining our programs?
The 2026 Chicago Language Symposium will discuss this and other questions on April 18, 2026. To make sure the symposium is accessible to presenters and participants across the country, it will be held entirely online via Zoom.
The Symposium is presented by the University of Chicago Language Center
together with:
The Council of Language Instruction and the Language Resource Center at Northwestern University
The Sandi Port Errant Language and Culture Learning Center at the University of Illinois-ChicagoThe Department of Modern Languages at DePaul University
Learn more about the Symposium below, and contact Cathy Baumann at ccbauman@uchicago.edu with questions. We sincerely hope you can join us.

title: Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
The storied city of Djenné, a center of Islamic learning, study, and scholarship since the twelfth century, is the hometown of Bamako-based photographer Hamdia Traoré (b. 1992, Mali). The thirty portraits in Traore’s series Des marabouts de Djenné (Marabouts of Jenne) reflect his intimate connections to the city’s people and deep history. Learned and devout, marabouts teach in Djenné’s over 50 Qur’anic schools, offer spiritual guidance, and treat ailments through their knowledge of the Qur’an.
Made during a time of political and social upheaval in Mali, these portraits reflect enduring cultural resilience. Each image depicts a marabout seated with the tools of his practice—books, Qur’an boards, amulets, and prayer beads—framed by the architecture and atmosphere of Djenné. The consistent format underscores their collective identity, while individual poses and captions highlight personal roles and neighborhoods.
Traoré’s work will be shown alongside mid-20th-century black-and-white portraits of marabouts by Malian photographers Mamadou Cissé, Abdourahmane Sakaly, and Tijani Sitou. Drawn from the Archive of Malian Photography, these earlier images share visual parallels and deepen the historical context. Together, these images illuminate evolving perspectives on spiritual authority, identity, and visual representation in Mali.

Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali” is curated by Kathleen Bickford Berzock, Associate Director of Curatorial Affairs, The Block Museum of Art, Northwestern University, in collaboration with Candace M. Keller, Associate Professor, Art History & Visual Culture, Michigan State University.  We are grateful to the Archive of Malian Photography (https://amp.matrix.msu.edu/), which provided digital scans of archival works. At The Block, the exhibition is supported by the Kadin/Spiegel Family Endowed Fund and a grant from the Illinois Arts Council.

title: Teresa Montoya’s Tó Łitso (Yellow Water): Ten Years after the Gold King Mine Spill
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
On August 5, 2015, the rupture of the abandoned Gold King Mine near Silverton, Colorado, released more than three million gallons of toxic wastewater into the Animas River, turning its waters a shocking shade of yellow. In the following year, artist and anthropologist Teresa Montoya (Diné, born 1984) embarked on a road trip from Silverton to Shiprock, New Mexico, retracing the path of the contaminated water and documenting its ongoing cultural, spiritual, and material effects on the Navajo Nation and other Indigenous communities downstream.
Marking the ten-year anniversary of the disaster, The Block Museum of Art partners with Montoya to revisit this journey. Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) explores the enduring consequences of the Gold King Mine spill through photography, sound recordings, water samples, and cartographic data.
Combining documentary photographs with scientific data and poetic reflection, Tó Łitso invites viewers to consider water not only as a life-sustaining resource but also as a conduit for histories, stories, and harm. In doing so, the exhibition challenges extractive frameworks of land use, centering Indigenous knowledge and resilience. Through Montoya’s interdisciplinary practice, Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) offers a powerful meditation on environmental and cultural justice in the Southwest and beyond.
"The 2015 spill had discharged more than three million gallons of acidic mine waste fluid into Cement Creek before joining the Animas River and eventually into the San Juan River, which flows directly across the Navajo Nation. Through my photographic journey, I make explicit the enduring presence of toxicity across multiple landscapes and territories. Sometimes it appears beautiful, other times haunting. The images highlight the relationships that various communities sustain through water/tó despite the occurrence of repeated and enduring contamination from upstream locales. The Gold King Mine Spill shows us this, even in that, which cannot be readily seen." – Teresa Montoya

title: Annual Meeting and Program: Songs for Spring
description:
Alliance Française du North Shore
**Annual Meeting for Alliance Française Members**
Required by Alliance Française du North Shore bylaws, this short members' meeting will convene at approximately 2:15 PM, immediately following the monthly program. This meeting will include approval of the proposed 2026-2027 budget and the election of members of the board of directors. Members of the AFduNS as of April 1 are eligible to vote in the election and on other matters.

If you're unable to attend the annual meeting, please complete this proxy ballot (link to come) **by 10 AM on Monday, April 20**.

**Program**
Singer and guitarist Kevin Soucie returns to share his extensive repertoire, including songs by Jacques Brel, Édith Piaf, Charles Trenet, Francis Cabrel, Charles Aznavour and many others. This intimate performance will transport you to the cafés of Paris with timeless melodies. [Click here for the program selections.](https://storage.googleapis.com/production-ipage-v1-0-0/830/1512830/9C8FaW1X/5b3eb831f2be4f27a020c51e7e705663?fileName=Music%20Program%202026.pdf) You won’t be able to help but sing along.

Kevin Soucie travels the country performing timeless, popular and contemporary French songs for francophone audiences. A native of Milwaukee, Soucie was a student in Québec and in Europe, and also served as a Peace Corps volunteer in Senegal. Hearing French and Québecois songs along the way, Soucie was inspired to sing and play *chansons* with his guitar—a hobby that became a passion.

**Program Registration for Members of the AF DuPage: Free**
If you're a member of the AF DuPage and are attending a monthly program for the first time, please RSVP to programmes@AFnorthshore.org with “DuPage” in the subject line. Include your name and Zoom screen name, if different. If you're a member of our Meetup group, we'll mark you as such and you'll no longer need to send RSVP emails as long as you RSVP on Meetup.
If you're not a member of the AFduNS Meetup group, you'll need to send an email RSVP for each monthly program.

**Program Registration for Nonmembers: $10**
If you're not a member of the AFduNS or the AF DuPage, the cost of attending this program is $10. [Click here](https://forms.gle/f89WGYhPkj867tYi8) to register and pay online with PayPal by 8 PM the day before the program. There will be no refunds or credits.

We appreciate reservations by 8 PM the day before the program. Walk-ins may pay at the door on their mobile device.

**Membership in the AFduNS**
While no membership is required for newcomers, we welcome new members whose membership will support the activities of the AFduNS, a nonprofit 501(c)(3) organization. For further info, visit our [website](https://afnorthshore.org/events) or [click here to contact us](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLScRxM03DpryO_lZlGWK-aDOCiFHFbivJimTScMOQmWk7KgOJQ/viewform?pli=1). If you wish to join the AFduNS, you may sign up [online](https://afnorthshore.org/join). Annual dues are $50.00.
