title: Movie Night
description: Small Things Like TheseBased on the book by Claire Keegan. Set in 1985 Christmastime in the Irish Village New Ross, the story follows coal merchant Bill Furlong, a hardworking father of five daughters. While delivering coal to a local convent, he witnesses harsh treatment of a young girl and later, while making an earlier than usual delivery to the convent, he discovers teenaged Sarah locked in the shed. These events lead to a flashback to his own difficult childhood, growing up the son of an unwed mother. What can he do in the present, especially in a small community that is afraid to challenge the status quo? Starring Cillian Murphy, Eileen Walsh, and Emily Watson.After watching the film, share your questions and comments in a discussion with Chris and Sharon.2024 : 98 minutes : Rated PG-13 for thematic material : Drama.Free tickets will be given to those who attend.Watch the trailer for Small Things Like These.Find Small Things Like These in the library catalog.

title: Between the Lines: The Funeral Ladies of Ellerie County by Claire Swinarski (Online)
description: Between the Lines meets for online discussions via Zoom. Registered patrons will receive an email the day before the program with the meeting ID and password for joining the event. New members are warmly welcome!Titles are always available on Hoopla, as well as other library ebook platforms. Contact us for assistance, or find any instructions you may need on our Download & Stream page.Book Synopsis: Armed with a Crock-Pot and a pile of recipes, a grandmother, her granddaughter, and a mysterious young man work to bring a community together in this uplifting novel for readers of The Chicken Sisters.Esther Larson has been cooking for funerals in the Northwoods of Wisconsin for seventy years. Known locally as the “funeral ladies,” she and her cohort have worked hard to keep the mourners of Ellerie County fed—it is her firm belief that there is very little a warm casserole and a piece of cherry pie can’t fix. But, after falling for an internet scam that puts her home at risk, the proud Larson family matriarch is the one in need of help these days.Iris, Esther’s whip-smart Gen Z granddaughter, would do anything for her family and her community. As she watches her friends and family move out of their lakeside town onto bigger and better things, Iris wonders why she feels so left behind in the place she is desperate to make her home. But when Cooper Welsh shows up, she finally starts to feel like she’s found the missing piece of her puzzle.Cooper is dealing with becoming a legal guardian to his younger half-sister after his beloved stepmother dies. While their celebrity-chef father is focused on his booming career and top-ranked television show, Cooper is still hurting from a public tragedy he witnessed last year as a paramedic and finding it hard to cope. With Iris in the gorgeous Ellerie County, though, he hopes he might finally find the home he’s been looking for.It doesn’t seem like a community cookbook could possibly solve their problems, especially one where casseroles have their own section and cream of chicken soup mix is the most frequently used ingredient. But when you mix the can-do spirit of Midwestern grandmothers with the stubborn hope of a boy raised by food plus a dash of long-awaited forgiveness—things might just turn out okay.

title: The Power of the Pen: Fiction Workshop | Sexual Assault Action Month
location: Women's Center, 2000 Sheridan Rd., Evanston, IL
description:
Writing fiction can provide an avenue to process and heal for many survivors of interpersonal violence. Incorporating themes and histories of harm into fictional narratives is a form of creativity and expression that may allow survivors to grow more resilient and access pathways to healing. This workshop will explore the ways survivors can use fiction writing to process their experiences and pursue healing. It will offer insights on how to pull from fact for the purposes of fiction writing and hold space for reflection and free writing.

Food provided | Registration Required

title: Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
The storied city of Djenné, a center of Islamic learning, study, and scholarship since the twelfth century, is the hometown of Bamako-based photographer Hamdia Traoré (b. 1992, Mali). The thirty portraits in Traore’s series Des marabouts de Djenné (Marabouts of Jenne) reflect his intimate connections to the city’s people and deep history. Learned and devout, marabouts teach in Djenné’s over 50 Qur’anic schools, offer spiritual guidance, and treat ailments through their knowledge of the Qur’an.
Made during a time of political and social upheaval in Mali, these portraits reflect enduring cultural resilience. Each image depicts a marabout seated with the tools of his practice—books, Qur’an boards, amulets, and prayer beads—framed by the architecture and atmosphere of Djenné. The consistent format underscores their collective identity, while individual poses and captions highlight personal roles and neighborhoods.
Traoré’s work will be shown alongside mid-20th-century black-and-white portraits of marabouts by Malian photographers Mamadou Cissé, Abdourahmane Sakaly, and Tijani Sitou. Drawn from the Archive of Malian Photography, these earlier images share visual parallels and deepen the historical context. Together, these images illuminate evolving perspectives on spiritual authority, identity, and visual representation in Mali.

Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali” is curated by Kathleen Bickford Berzock, Associate Director of Curatorial Affairs, The Block Museum of Art, Northwestern University, in collaboration with Candace M. Keller, Associate Professor, Art History & Visual Culture, Michigan State University.  We are grateful to the Archive of Malian Photography (https://amp.matrix.msu.edu/), which provided digital scans of archival works. At The Block, the exhibition is supported by the Kadin/Spiegel Family Endowed Fund and a grant from the Illinois Arts Council.

title: Teresa Montoya’s Tó Łitso (Yellow Water): Ten Years after the Gold King Mine Spill
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
On August 5, 2015, the rupture of the abandoned Gold King Mine near Silverton, Colorado, released more than three million gallons of toxic wastewater into the Animas River, turning its waters a shocking shade of yellow. In the following year, artist and anthropologist Teresa Montoya (Diné, born 1984) embarked on a road trip from Silverton to Shiprock, New Mexico, retracing the path of the contaminated water and documenting its ongoing cultural, spiritual, and material effects on the Navajo Nation and other Indigenous communities downstream.
Marking the ten-year anniversary of the disaster, The Block Museum of Art partners with Montoya to revisit this journey. Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) explores the enduring consequences of the Gold King Mine spill through photography, sound recordings, water samples, and cartographic data.
Combining documentary photographs with scientific data and poetic reflection, Tó Łitso invites viewers to consider water not only as a life-sustaining resource but also as a conduit for histories, stories, and harm. In doing so, the exhibition challenges extractive frameworks of land use, centering Indigenous knowledge and resilience. Through Montoya’s interdisciplinary practice, Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) offers a powerful meditation on environmental and cultural justice in the Southwest and beyond.
"The 2015 spill had discharged more than three million gallons of acidic mine waste fluid into Cement Creek before joining the Animas River and eventually into the San Juan River, which flows directly across the Navajo Nation. Through my photographic journey, I make explicit the enduring presence of toxicity across multiple landscapes and territories. Sometimes it appears beautiful, other times haunting. The images highlight the relationships that various communities sustain through water/tó despite the occurrence of repeated and enduring contamination from upstream locales. The Gold King Mine Spill shows us this, even in that, which cannot be readily seen." – Teresa Montoya

title: ISITA | The Chronicles of Two West African Kingdoms - Zachary Wright, Ali Diakite, Mauro Nobili
location: Harris Hall, 1881 Sheridan Road, Evanston, IL
description:
Join the Institute for the Study of Islamic Thought in Africa (ISITA) for a talk in our Islam in Africa - New Books in the Field series.
Lunch will be served at the talk.
Mauro Nobili, Zachary Wright, and Ali Diakite will discuss their new book, The Chronicles of Two West African Kingdoms: TheTārīkh Ibn Al-Mukhtār of the Songhay Empire and the Tārīkh Al-Fattāsh of the Caliphate of Ḥamdallāhi (The British Academy / Liverpool University Press, 2025).
Description: The Chronicles of Two West African Kingdoms presents a new reading of West African history from the fifteenth to the nineteenth centuries. Based on close analysis of Arabic manuscripts from Timbuktu and other regions, this volume provides key historical context from the Songhay Empire through the Caliphate of Ḥamdallāhi, along with translations and Arabic editions of the long-obscured Tārīkh Ibn al-Mukhtār in parallel with the later production known as the Tārīkh al-fattāsh. The central observation that emerges from these texts is that Muslim scholars in premodern West Africa, who saw themselves as constitutive to the powerful kingdoms in the Western Sudan, claimed almost unprecedented authority to shape reality through narration.
Dr. Mauro Nobili is Professor of History at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign.
Dr. Ali Diakite is Cataloger for West African Manuscripts at the Hill Museum and Manuscript Library (Collegeville, MN).
Dr. Zachary Wright is ISITA's Director, as well as Professor in Residence and Associate Dean for Faculty Affairs at Northwestern University in Qatar.

title: Writing Brown Crafts: A Creative Praxis for Our Present | A talk by Dr. Christopher Patterson
location: Crowe Hall, 1860 Campus Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
This talk reflects on acts of writing in times of grief, disillusionment, and emerging fascism. Christopher B. Patterson will discuss their newly published book, Domesticating Brown: Movements of Racial Imagination, as an example of “brown craft” authorship that seeks solidarity through undisciplined critical methods, merging academic writing with fiction, poetry, and interactive video games. Patterson considers the unique stages of their own creative and academic work through themes of mixed brownness: neurodivergence, queerness, Filipinx American colonial history, and creative resistance. These means of forming “brown crafts” have the potential to refuse narratives of creative acts themselves as a resolution to imperial oppression, directing attention instead to the forms of solidarity, action, and radical imaginings that such works make possible.

Christopher B. Patterson is Professor of The Social Justice Institute at The University of British Columbia. Their research focuses on literature, video games, and new media through the lens of transpacific empire studies, critical race theory, and queer theory. They are the award-winning author of seven books, which include the prose-poetry collection Nimrods: a fake-punk self-hurt anti-memoir (Duke University Press, 2023), the essay collection Of Floating Isles: On Growing Pains and Video Games (Arsenal Pulp Press, 2025), and the academic book Domesticating Brown: Movements of Racial Imagination (Duke University Press, 2026). They are the co-editor of two anthologies published in 2024: Transpacific, Undisciplined (University of Washington Press), and Made in Asia/America: Why Video Games Were Never (Really) About Us (Duke University Press), and the lead designer of the video game Stamped: an anti-travel game, an adaptation of their first novel. They write creative work under their matrilineal name Kawika Guillermo, and they have lived in Portland, Las Vegas, Seattle, Gimhae South Korea, Nanjing China, Hong Kong, and Vancouver Canada.

"The 2015 spill had discharged more than three million gallons of acidic mine waste fluid into Cement Creek before joining the Animas River and eventually into the San Juan River, which flows directly across the Navajo Nation. Through my photographic journey, I make explicit the enduring presence of toxicity across multiple landscapes and territories. Sometimes it appears beautiful, other times haunting. The images highlight the relationships that various communities sustain through water/tó despite the occurrence of repeated and enduring contamination from upstream locales. title: MGPL After Dark: The Librarians
location: 6140 Lincoln Avenue, Morton Grove, IL, 60053, US
description: In this award winning documentary, librarians across the US examine how book review policies and collection standards that restrict race-related and LGBTQIA+ content are shaping education and communities.Join us for a special screening of this documentary as part of National Library Week. Light refreshments will be served at 6:00 pm. More about the film: When Texas lawmakers seek to review a list of books, librarians find themselves on the frontlines of a national battle. Across the U.S., librarians face the impact of uniting against library collection standards that include restrictions on race-related and LGBTQIA+ content. Drawing on historical context, The Librarians explores the broader implications for education and public life. (2025, NR, 1hr 35min) About Indie Lens Pop-UpIndie Lens Pop-Up is a community series that brings people together for film screenings and conversations. Featuring documentaries seen on PBS's Independent Lens, Indie Lens Pop-Up draws local residents, leaders, and organizations to discuss what matters most. For more information, visit pbs.org/indielenspopup.

title: Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
The storied city of Djenné, a center of Islamic learning, study, and scholarship since the twelfth century, is the hometown of Bamako-based photographer Hamdia Traoré (b. 1992, Mali). The thirty portraits in Traore’s series Des marabouts de Djenné (Marabouts of Jenne) reflect his intimate connections to the city’s people and deep history. Learned and devout, marabouts teach in Djenné’s over 50 Qur’anic schools, offer spiritual guidance, and treat ailments through their knowledge of the Qur’an.
Made during a time of political and social upheaval in Mali, these portraits reflect enduring cultural resilience. Each image depicts a marabout seated with the tools of his practice—books, Qur’an boards, amulets, and prayer beads—framed by the architecture and atmosphere of Djenné. The consistent format underscores their collective identity, while individual poses and captions highlight personal roles and neighborhoods.
Traoré’s work will be shown alongside mid-20th-century black-and-white portraits of marabouts by Malian photographers Mamadou Cissé, Abdourahmane Sakaly, and Tijani Sitou. Drawn from the Archive of Malian Photography, these earlier images share visual parallels and deepen the historical context. Together, these images illuminate evolving perspectives on spiritual authority, identity, and visual representation in Mali.

Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali” is curated by Kathleen Bickford Berzock, Associate Director of Curatorial Affairs, The Block Museum of Art, Northwestern University, in collaboration with Candace M. Keller, Associate Professor, Art History & Visual Culture, Michigan State University.  We are grateful to the Archive of Malian Photography (https://amp.matrix.msu.edu/), which provided digital scans of archival works. At The Block, the exhibition is supported by the Kadin/Spiegel Family Endowed Fund and a grant from the Illinois Arts Council.

title: Teresa Montoya’s Tó Łitso (Yellow Water): Ten Years after the Gold King Mine Spill
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
On August 5, 2015, the rupture of the abandoned Gold King Mine near Silverton, Colorado, released more than three million gallons of toxic wastewater into the Animas River, turning its waters a shocking shade of yellow. In the following year, artist and anthropologist Teresa Montoya (Diné, born 1984) embarked on a road trip from Silverton to Shiprock, New Mexico, retracing the path of the contaminated water and documenting its ongoing cultural, spiritual, and material effects on the Navajo Nation and other Indigenous communities downstream.
Marking the ten-year anniversary of the disaster, The Block Museum of Art partners with Montoya to revisit this journey. Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) explores the enduring consequences of the Gold King Mine spill through photography, sound recordings, water samples, and cartographic data.
Combining documentary photographs with scientific data and poetic reflection, Tó Łitso invites viewers to consider water not only as a life-sustaining resource but also as a conduit for histories, stories, and harm. In doing so, the exhibition challenges extractive frameworks of land use, centering Indigenous knowledge and resilience. Through Montoya’s interdisciplinary practice, Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) offers a powerful meditation on environmental and cultural justice in the Southwest and beyond.
"The 2015 spill had discharged more than three million gallons of acidic mine waste fluid into Cement Creek before joining the Animas River and eventually into the San Juan River, which flows directly across the Navajo Nation. Through my photographic journey, I make explicit the enduring presence of toxicity across multiple landscapes and territories. Sometimes it appears beautiful, other times haunting. The images highlight the relationships that various communities sustain through water/tó despite the occurrence of repeated and enduring contamination from upstream locales. The Gold King Mine Spill shows us this, even in that, which cannot be readily seen." – Teresa Montoya

title: Marquis Hill
location: Pick-Staiger Concert Hall, 50 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
“His genre exchange has the ease of a walk across the street to a friend’s place.”
—Downbeat
Trumpeter and composer Marquis Hill has made it his mission to break down the barriers between contemporary and classic jazz, hip-hop, R&B, Chicago house, and neo-soul. The Chicago native and 2014 winner of the Thelonious Monk Institute of Jazz Competition has performed as a member of the Chicago Jazz Orchestra, an in-demand sideman, and a bandleader, earning accolades with his working band, the Blacktet. His collaborations have included such artists as Marcus Miller, Dee Dee Bridgewater, Boney James, Kurt Elling, Joe Lovano, and Makaya McCraven. Hill’s latest album, (Beautifulism) Sweet Surrender, was released on March 13.
This performance is sponsored in part by WDCB 90.9 FM.

title: Aristo Sham, piano
location: Patrick G. and Shirley W. Ryan Center for the Musical Arts, 70 Arts Circle, Evanston, IL
description:
Part of the Skyline Piano Artist Series
Winner of the Gold Medal at the 2025 Van Cliburn International Piano Competition, Aristo Sham is a laureate of international competitions, with first-prize wins at Ettlingen, Gina Bachauer, and the Monte Carlo Music Masters, Sham has played across Asia, Europe, and the United States with the likes of the London Symphony Orchestra, Hong Kong Philharmonic, and the Orchestre de Chambre de Lausanne. The son of a piano teacher, Sham began lessons at age 3 and was competing and concertizing by age 10. Soon after, he was featured in the UK Channel 4 documentary The World’s Greatest Musical Prodigies. Balancing piano with other interests, Sham earned his Bachelor of Arts degree in Economics from Harvard University and a Master of Music degree from the New England Conservatory. He proceeded to study at the Ingesund School of Music in Sweden and earn an artist diploma from the Juilliard School. While pursuing a career as a pianist, Sham continues to enjoy other interests such as travel, aviation, gastronomy, oenology, and language.

title: #114: Nervous Conditions by Tsitsi Dangarembga
description:
Black Girls Read Book Club
**Our April book selection is *Nervous Conditions* by Tsitsi Dangarembga!**

About the book: The groundbreaking first novel in Tsitsi Dangarembga’s award-winning trilogy, *Nervous Conditions*, won the Commonwealth Writers Prize and has been “hailed as one of the 20th century’s most significant works of African literature” (*The New York Times*). Two decades before Zimbabwe would win independence and ended white minority rule, thirteen-year-old Tambudzai Sigauke embarks on her education. On her shoulders rest the economic hopes of her parents, siblings, and extended family, and within her burns the desire for independence. She yearns to be free of the constraints of her rural village and thinks she’s found her way out when her wealthy uncle offers to sponsor her schooling. But she soon learns that the education she receives at his mission school comes with a price.

**Support Black Girls Read Chicago by purchasing *[Nervous Conditions](https://bookshop.org/a/25397/9781644450710)* on Bookshop (affiliate link).**

title: Civic Love
description: Outside in our south courtyard with cozy fire vibes, see how civic love appears in your life and how you can strengthen relationships in your community.36 Questions for Civic Love is a tool created by the National Public Housing Museum (NPHM), based on psychologist Arthur Aron and Detroit-based organizer Grace Lee Boggs’ work. According to the NPHM, “Civic love is one’s love for society, expressed through a commitment to the common good. It is a belief in the idea that we’re all better off, when we are all better off." In this event, you will connect with friends and neighbors and learn how you can experience and grow the practice of civic love. Keyword: USA 250

Though Vanna and Amir are complete strangers, though they don't speak a common language, Vanna is determined to do whatever it takes to save the boy.Registration required to receive Zoom information.

title: Readpool: Salamandre by I.N.J. Culbard (Online)
description: Readpool is an adult graphic novel discussion group with a focus on challenging and acclaimed stories.Book Synopsis: Kaspar Salamandre is a bereaved young artist who is sent to stay with his enigmatic grandfather in a land ruled under an oppressive regime, where there can be only one loved one: The Emperor. In this land where flowers are contraband, music is illegal, and art is created in hiding, Kaspar discovers a world of art revolutionaries, espionage and the Secret Police. His search for answers will bring him face to face with the meaning of sacrifice. But, will anything bring him closer to overcoming his loss?If you need assistance with getting a copy of the book, please contact Nate Miller at nmiller@mgpl.org or at 847-929-5115.

Over the course of a career spanning nearly five decades, Davis remained at the vanguard of multiple stylistic developments in jazz, including bebop, cool jazz, hard bop, modal jazz, and fusion.

title: Online Apéro Conversation
description:
Alliance Française du North Shore
Meet fellow Francophones for evening conversation. This gathering is facilitated by one or more of our native or fluent AFduNS leaders.

Pour yourself a glass of wine, gather some snacks and settle in at home for our online conversation group.

The meeting will be held on Zoom. Although you don't need to download Zoom to attend this meeting, we encourage you to do so (https://zoom.us/download) for a smoother experience. Depending on the number of attendees, we may break out into smaller groups.

The link to the meeting will be visible when you RSVP. The meeting ID is 859 8255 4800.

All fluency levels are welcome. RSVPs help us plan.

**Membership in the AFduNS**
While no membership is required for newcomers, we welcome new members whose membership will support the activities of the AFduNS, a nonprofit 501(c)(3) organization. For further info, visit our [website](https://afnorthshore.org/events) or [click here to contact us](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLScRxM03DpryO_lZlGWK-aDOCiFHFbivJimTScMOQmWk7KgOJQ/viewform?pli=1). If you wish to join the AFduNS, you may sign up [online](https://afnorthshore.org/join). Annual dues are $50.00.

description: Have you ever wanted to be part of a library book discussion group, but you worried that your English language skills were not strong enough?In this class for advanced-level English learners, we will read and discuss an American novel together, with the support of Margaret Feit Clarke, an experienced ESL teacher from the ELL Parent Center. You will strengthen your English skills while learning more about great literature, American culture, and the Skokie community. Limited to 16 participants. Free tickets will be given to those who attend.The class will meet on Tuesdays for 12 weeks: March 3-May 12, 9:30-11:30 am. No class March 31.We’ll read and talk about Olive, Again by Elizabeth Strout. The book offers a series of interconnected stories that provide a portrait of a complicated woman confronting her own mortality.Find Olive, Again in the library catalog.Find additional titles by Elizabeth Strout in the library catalog.

title: A Tribute to Amy Winehouse by Rachel Drew
Historical Examinations of Women in Western Civilizations in the Second World War and Beyond: A Virtual Conference
description:
Over two evenings, this conference explores women's experiences in Western Civilizations during and after the Second World War. The examination will include several countries and situations.

The conference starts on April 29, 2026, at 6 p.m. with introductory remarks by Wendy Adele-Marie, Ph.D., Distinguished Professor of History and Coordinator of Jewish Studies at Oakton College.  After each evening session, there will be time for Q&A. Rija Quereshi will deliver the closing statement at the end of the conference on April 30.

The conference proceedings will be published in Adele-Marie’s forthcoming second edition of her OER workbook, History of Women in Western Civilizations. Oakton will also offer the course Women in Western Civilizations (HIS 236) online and asynchronously in the summer of 2026 to further complement the conference.

Please register in advance to attend the two-day virtual conference; the same link serves for both nights. All of the times for the event are in Chicago time. You can check time zones to help you register. Please note that the times of each session are subject to change. For more information about this event, please contact the conference organizer, Wendy Adele-Marie, at wadelemarie@oakton.edu.

This event is sponsored by Jewish Studies and the EGHPS Department at Oakton College. This event is also made possible by a generous grant from the Oakton Educational Foundation.

title: Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
The storied city of Djenné, a center of Islamic learning, study, and scholarship since the twelfth century, is the hometown of Bamako-based photographer Hamdia Traoré (b. 1992, Mali). The thirty portraits in Traore’s series Des marabouts de Djenné (Marabouts of Jenne) reflect his intimate connections to the city’s people and deep history. Learned and devout, marabouts teach in Djenné’s over 50 Qur’anic schools, offer spiritual guidance, and treat ailments through their knowledge of the Qur’an.
Made during a time of political and social upheaval in Mali, these portraits reflect enduring cultural resilience. Each image depicts a marabout seated with the tools of his practice—books, Qur’an boards, amulets, and prayer beads—framed by the architecture and atmosphere of Djenné. The consistent format underscores their collective identity, while individual poses and captions highlight personal roles and neighborhoods.
Traoré’s work will be shown alongside mid-20th-century black-and-white portraits of marabouts by Malian photographers Mamadou Cissé, Abdourahmane Sakaly, and Tijani Sitou. Drawn from the Archive of Malian Photography, these earlier images share visual parallels and deepen the historical context. Together, these images illuminate evolving perspectives on spiritual authority, identity, and visual representation in Mali.

Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali” is curated by Kathleen Bickford Berzock, Associate Director of Curatorial Affairs, The Block Museum of Art, Northwestern University, in collaboration with Candace M. Keller, Associate Professor, Art History & Visual Culture, Michigan State University.  We are grateful to the Archive of Malian Photography (https://amp.matrix.msu.edu/), which provided digital scans of archival works. At The Block, the exhibition is supported by the Kadin/Spiegel Family Endowed Fund and a grant from the Illinois Arts Council.

title: Teresa Montoya’s Tó Łitso (Yellow Water): Ten Years after the Gold King Mine Spill
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
On August 5, 2015, the rupture of the abandoned Gold King Mine near Silverton, Colorado, released more than three million gallons of toxic wastewater into the Animas River, turning its waters a shocking shade of yellow. In the following year, artist and anthropologist Teresa Montoya (Diné, born 1984) embarked on a road trip from Silverton to Shiprock, New Mexico, retracing the path of the contaminated water and documenting its ongoing cultural, spiritual, and material effects on the Navajo Nation and other Indigenous communities downstream.
Marking the ten-year anniversary of the disaster, The Block Museum of Art partners with Montoya to revisit this journey. Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) explores the enduring consequences of the Gold King Mine spill through photography, sound recordings, water samples, and cartographic data.
Combining documentary photographs with scientific data and poetic reflection, Tó Łitso invites viewers to consider water not only as a life-sustaining resource but also as a conduit for histories, stories, and harm. In doing so, the exhibition challenges extractive frameworks of land use, centering Indigenous knowledge and resilience. Through Montoya’s interdisciplinary practice, Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) offers a powerful meditation on environmental and cultural justice in the Southwest and beyond.
"The 2015 spill had discharged more than three million gallons of acidic mine waste fluid into Cement Creek before joining the Animas River and eventually into the San Juan River, which flows directly across the Navajo Nation. Through my photographic journey, I make explicit the enduring presence of toxicity across multiple landscapes and territories. Sometimes it appears beautiful, other times haunting. The images highlight the relationships that various communities sustain through water/tó despite the occurrence of repeated and enduring contamination from upstream locales. The Gold King Mine Spill shows us this, even in that, which cannot be readily seen." – Teresa Montoya

title: Northwestern University Jazz Orchestra
location: Patrick G. and Shirley W. Ryan Center for the Musical Arts, 70 Arts Circle, Evanston, IL
description:
Derrick Gardner, conductor; Ari Hoenig, drums
The Jazz Orchestra welcomes guest drummer Ari Hoenig for their first concert of the spring quarter. Dubbed a “postbop daredevil” (Time Out New York) for whom “rhythm is a contact sport” (New York Times), Hoenig has performed with the Kenny Werner Trio, the Chris Potter Underground, and the Joshua Redman Elastic Band, as well as bands led by Wayne Krantz, Mike Stern, Richard Bona, and Pat Martino. He has led the Ari Hoenig Quartet since 2002 and is a faculty member at New York University and the New School for Social Research.

title: Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
The storied city of Djenné, a center of Islamic learning, study, and scholarship since the twelfth century, is the hometown of Bamako-based photographer Hamdia Traoré (b. 1992, Mali). The thirty portraits in Traore’s series Des marabouts de Djenné (Marabouts of Jenne) reflect his intimate connections to the city’s people and deep history. Learned and devout, marabouts teach in Djenné’s over 50 Qur’anic schools, offer spiritual guidance, and treat ailments through their knowledge of the Qur’an.
Made during a time of political and social upheaval in Mali, these portraits reflect enduring cultural resilience. Each image depicts a marabout seated with the tools of his practice—books, Qur’an boards, amulets, and prayer beads—framed by the architecture and atmosphere of Djenné. The consistent format underscores their collective identity, while individual poses and captions highlight personal roles and neighborhoods.
Traoré’s work will be shown alongside mid-20th-century black-and-white portraits of marabouts by Malian photographers Mamadou Cissé, Abdourahmane Sakaly, and Tijani Sitou. Drawn from the Archive of Malian Photography, these earlier images share visual parallels and deepen the historical context. Together, these images illuminate evolving perspectives on spiritual authority, identity, and visual representation in Mali.

Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali” is curated by Kathleen Bickford Berzock, Associate Director of Curatorial Affairs, The Block Museum of Art, Northwestern University, in collaboration with Candace M. Keller, Associate Professor, Art History & Visual Culture, Michigan State University.  We are grateful to the Archive of Malian Photography (https://amp.matrix.msu.edu/), which provided digital scans of archival works. At The Block, the exhibition is supported by the Kadin/Spiegel Family Endowed Fund and a grant from the Illinois Arts Council.

title: Symphonic Wind Ensemble and Combined Choirs: Sacred and Profane
location: Pick-Staiger Concert Hall, 50 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
Andrew Megill, A. J. Keller, and Robert Taylor, conductors
Combined Bienen School choirs and the Symphonic Wind Ensemble join forces for a unique collaboration exploring music of awe, reverence, and fate. The program opens with Randall Thompson’s stirring The Last Words of David and Anton Bruckner’s monumental Mass No. 2 in E Minor. Brahms’s noble Begräbnisgesang (Burial Song) offers a moment of reflective solemnity, setting the stage for the raw energy of Carl Orff’s Carmina Burana in its thrilling arrangement for chorus and wind ensemble. This unforgettable night showcases the full expressive spectrum of voices and winds from the sacred to the elemental.
Randall Thompson, The Last Words of David
Anton Bruckner, Mass No. 2 in E Minor, WAB 27
Johannes Brahms, Begräbnisgesang (Burial Song), Op. 13
Carl Orff (trans. Juan Vicente Mas Quiles), Carmina Burana, Part 1

title: America's Oddest Museums - Film Screening
description: Join multi-Emmy Award winning producer and editor Harvey Moshman for a screening of a 1-hour special of the PBS series Wild Travels. When it comes to museums, there’s the kind your school made you go to on field trips, but what about the quirkier variety that feature collections of mustard, or pinball machines, or even just dust; that showcase action figures, or portraits made entirely from bugs? The kind of museums that are located in motel lobbies, or in homemade castles, or abandoned barns? Learn about them all in AMERICA'S ODDEST MUSEUMS. Screening followed by live Q&A.

title: Symphonic Band
location: Pick-Staiger Concert Hall, 50 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
Shawn Vondran, conductor
The centerpiece of the Symphonic Band’s first spring concert is Modest Mussorgsky’s Pictures at an Exhibition (orchestrated by Maurice Ravel and transcribed for band by Paul Lavender). The pictures the work depicts were created by Mussorgsky’s friend, artist Viktor Hartmann. Following Hartmann’s untimely death, the Imperial Academy of Arts in Saint Petersburg exhibited his work, and after touring the exhibition, Mussorgsky was inspired to compose the score.
Johan Halvorsen (arr. Frederick Fennell), Bojarernes intogsmarsch (Entry March of the Boyars)
Eric Whitacre, Lux Aurumque
Dennis Llinás, La Chancla
Modest Mussorgsky (orch. Maurice Ravel, trans. Paul Lavender), Pictures at an Exhibition

title: Young Steinway Concert
description: Alison Chung, a senior at Glenbrook North High School, studies violin with Desirée Ruhstrat. Alison was recently named runner-up in the ADM Concerto Competition and performed the first movement of the Walton Violin Concerto with the Kishwaukee Symphony Orchestra. As a member of Quartetto Speranza, she received the Bronze Medal in the Junior Division at the 2025 Fischoff National Chamber Music Competition. With the Phoenix Quartet, she earned second prize at the 2026 Rembrandt Chamber Music Competition and first prize in the Open Division of the Chicago National Chamber Music Competition.Duo Dunamis consists of pianists David Goetz and Ryan Hu. David is a junior at Loyola Academy and Ryan is a junior at North Shore Country Day School. Both have been awarded first place for both duo and solo performance in the American Protege Music Competition and performed at Weill Recital Hall at Carnegie Hall. The duo has appeared in the Chicago Duo Piano Music Festival, the Plymouth Concert Series, and the Merion Concert Series.Pianist Pratyay Rao is a junior at New Trier Township High School. He studies piano with Lyudmila Lakisova. Pratyay won second place in the 2025 American Fine Arts Festival International Concerto Competition, was a finalist in 2024 New Trier Concerto Competition, and won first place in the 2024 Charleston Summer Music Competition. Pratyay also participates in Science Olympiad and DECA competitions.Free tickets will be given to those present starting at 2:30 pm.

title: Morning Movie
description: Elio Eleven-year-old Elio believes that extraterrestrials are out there and feels he’d fit in better with them. One day, his dream comes true: he's taken to the Communiverse and is mistaken for an ambassador from Earth. This fun, action-packed cosmic misadventure explores themes of loneliness, belonging, grief, loss, family, acceptance, self-discovery, and identity. Elio and his space-alien best friend Glordon are endearing and relatable, and the animation is bright and imaginative. Featuring the voice talents of Yonas Kibreab, Remy Edgerly, Zoe Saldana, Brad Garrett, Jameela Jamil, Shirley Henderson, and Matthias Schweighofer. Directed by Madeline Sharafian, Domee Shi, and Adrian Molina. This received an Oscar nomination for Best Animated Feature Film. 2025 : 98 minutes : Rated PG for some action/peril and thematic elements : AnimationFree tickets will be given to all who attend. Watch the trailer for Elio.Find Elio in the library catalog.

description: This two-part series invites you to practice ways to have respectful conversation across differences, facilitated by library staff using the same approaches we use every day at the library. In the second session (May 11), you’ll build on the skills from the previous session and practice holding conversations. Light refreshments will be provided.

title: Jazz Small Ensembles: Countdown to 100—The Music of John Coltrane
location: Patrick G. and Shirley W. Ryan Center for the Musical Arts, 70 Arts Circle, Evanston, IL
description:
Darius Hampton and Joe Clark, conductors
Saxophonist, bandleader, and composer John Coltrane is among the most influential figures in the history of jazz and 20th-century music. He began his career working in the bebop and hard bop styles, eventually helping to pioneer the use of modes and becoming a leader in the development of free jazz. As Coltrane’s September 23 centennial anniversary approaches, the Darius Hampton and Joe Clark ensembles pay tribute to his legacy.

Limited to 16 participants. Free tickets will be given to those who attend.The class will meet on Tuesdays for 12 weeks: March 3-May 12, 9:30-11:30 am. No class March 31.We’ll read and talk about Olive, Again by Elizabeth Strout. title: Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
The storied city of Djenné, a center of Islamic learning, study, and scholarship since the twelfth century, is the hometown of Bamako-based photographer Hamdia Traoré (b. 1992, Mali). The thirty portraits in Traore’s series Des marabouts de Djenné (Marabouts of Jenne) reflect his intimate connections to the city’s people and deep history. Learned and devout, marabouts teach in Djenné’s over 50 Qur’anic schools, offer spiritual guidance, and treat ailments through their knowledge of the Qur’an.
Made during a time of political and social upheaval in Mali, these portraits reflect enduring cultural resilience. Each image depicts a marabout seated with the tools of his practice—books, Qur’an boards, amulets, and prayer beads—framed by the architecture and atmosphere of Djenné. The consistent format underscores their collective identity, while individual poses and captions highlight personal roles and neighborhoods.
Traoré’s work will be shown alongside mid-20th-century black-and-white portraits of marabouts by Malian photographers Mamadou Cissé, Abdourahmane Sakaly, and Tijani Sitou. Drawn from the Archive of Malian Photography, these earlier images share visual parallels and deepen the historical context. Together, these images illuminate evolving perspectives on spiritual authority, identity, and visual representation in Mali.

Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali” is curated by Kathleen Bickford Berzock, Associate Director of Curatorial Affairs, The Block Museum of Art, Northwestern University, in collaboration with Candace M. Keller, Associate Professor, Art History & Visual Culture, Michigan State University.  We are grateful to the Archive of Malian Photography (https://amp.matrix.msu.edu/), which provided digital scans of archival works. At The Block, the exhibition is supported by the Kadin/Spiegel Family Endowed Fund and a grant from the Illinois Arts Council.

title: Teresa Montoya’s Tó Łitso (Yellow Water): Ten Years after the Gold King Mine Spill
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
On August 5, 2015, the rupture of the abandoned Gold King Mine near Silverton, Colorado, released more than three million gallons of toxic wastewater into the Animas River, turning its waters a shocking shade of yellow. In the following year, artist and anthropologist Teresa Montoya (Diné, born 1984) embarked on a road trip from Silverton to Shiprock, New Mexico, retracing the path of the contaminated water and documenting its ongoing cultural, spiritual, and material effects on the Navajo Nation and other Indigenous communities downstream.
Marking the ten-year anniversary of the disaster, The Block Museum of Art partners with Montoya to revisit this journey. Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) explores the enduring consequences of the Gold King Mine spill through photography, sound recordings, water samples, and cartographic data.
Combining documentary photographs with scientific data and poetic reflection, Tó Łitso invites viewers to consider water not only as a life-sustaining resource but also as a conduit for histories, stories, and harm. In doing so, the exhibition challenges extractive frameworks of land use, centering Indigenous knowledge and resilience. Through Montoya’s interdisciplinary practice, Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) offers a powerful meditation on environmental and cultural justice in the Southwest and beyond.
"The 2015 spill had discharged more than three million gallons of acidic mine waste fluid into Cement Creek before joining the Animas River and eventually into the San Juan River, which flows directly across the Navajo Nation. Through my photographic journey, I make explicit the enduring presence of toxicity across multiple landscapes and territories. Sometimes it appears beautiful, other times haunting. The images highlight the relationships that various communities sustain through water/tó despite the occurrence of repeated and enduring contamination from upstream locales. The Gold King Mine Spill shows us this, even in that, which cannot be readily seen." – Teresa Montoya

title: Past and Future Feminist Fiction: Gina Frangello and Elizabeth Crane in conversation with Megan Stielstra
location: Alice Millar Chapel, 1870 Sheridan Road, Evanston, IL
description:
The Women’s Center and Northwestern University Press are proud to host celebrated authors, Elizabeth Crane (The History of Great Things, This Story Will Change:After the Happily Ever After and We Only Know So Much) and Gina Frangello (Blow Your House Down: A Story of Family, Feminism, and Treason, Every Kind of Wanting: A Novel, and A Life in Men: A Novel) in conversation with Megan Stielstra (Everyone Remain Calm, Once I Was Cool and The Wrong Way to Save Your Life).This April NU Press is publishing Crane’s much -anticipated new collection of short stories exploring themes of aging, sex, and friendship, alongside a reissue of her out-of-print first collection, When the Messenger is Hot. In February, NUPress published reprints of Frangello’s celebrated first two books, My Sister’s Continent: A Novel and Slut Lullabies: Stories. This spring is thereby the perfect opportunity to engage these incredible feminist writers in a candid conversation about sex, relationships, the what now of #MeToo, and what they find looking back through their own legacies of feminist writing.

Book sales and reception in the courtyard to follow.

title: Walking Chicago's Coast: A 63-Mile Journey to the Indiana Dunes

He will also show slides from his two-day walk across Chicago. Participants will have the chance to ask questions and share their experiences about the benefits of walking.

title: Love & Information
location: Wirtz Center: Ethel M. Barber Theater, 30 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
By Caryl Churchill
Directed by Claire Bauman
Someone sneezes. Someone can’t get a signal. Someone won’t answer the door. Someone put an elephant on the stairs. Someone’s not ready to talk. Someone is her brother’s mother. Someone hates irrational numbers. Someone told the police. Someone got a message from the traffic light. Someone’s never felt like this before.
In this fast-moving kaleidoscope, more than a hundred characters try to make sense of what they know.
Approximate run time: 1 hour and 45 minutes, no intermission
Suggested Ages: 13+

title: Friday Film
description: Song Sung BlueBased on an inspiring true story about Mike and Claire Sardina, a Milwaukee couple who formed the popular Neil Diamond tribute band called Lightning & Thunder. The movie follows their joyful, soaring successes, devastating setbacks, and, most of all, their enduring love for one another. Starring Hugh Jackman and Kate Hudson in her Oscar-nominated portrayal of Clair Sardina. By Caryl Churchill

Someone sneezes. Someone can’t get a signal. Someone won’t answer the door. Someone put an elephant on the stairs. Someone’s not ready to talk. Someone is her brother’s mother. Someone hates irrational numbers. Someone told the police. Someone got a message from the traffic light. Someone’s never felt like this before.
In this fast-moving kaleidoscope, more than a hundred characters try to make sense of what they know.

The redhaired mythology that glorifies and empowers two women leads them into a big love, but can’t lead them safely out again. A play about the heaviness of the things we carry.

Our walk will be easy, slow-paced, and educational, and hopefully we will see lots of birds! Bring binoculars if you have them, but if you don't, that's no problem.Please meet us at the north end of the parking lot off of Willow Road here:https://maps.app.goo.gl/W1EAkqmLoonUWQyY6

title: Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
The storied city of Djenné, a center of Islamic learning, study, and scholarship since the twelfth century, is the hometown of Bamako-based photographer Hamdia Traoré (b. 1992, Mali). The thirty portraits in Traore’s series Des marabouts de Djenné (Marabouts of Jenne) reflect his intimate connections to the city’s people and deep history. Learned and devout, marabouts teach in Djenné’s over 50 Qur’anic schools, offer spiritual guidance, and treat ailments through their knowledge of the Qur’an.
Made during a time of political and social upheaval in Mali, these portraits reflect enduring cultural resilience. Each image depicts a marabout seated with the tools of his practice—books, Qur’an boards, amulets, and prayer beads—framed by the architecture and atmosphere of Djenné. The consistent format underscores their collective identity, while individual poses and captions highlight personal roles and neighborhoods.
Traoré’s work will be shown alongside mid-20th-century black-and-white portraits of marabouts by Malian photographers Mamadou Cissé, Abdourahmane Sakaly, and Tijani Sitou. Drawn from the Archive of Malian Photography, these earlier images share visual parallels and deepen the historical context. Together, these images illuminate evolving perspectives on spiritual authority, identity, and visual representation in Mali.

Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali” is curated by Kathleen Bickford Berzock, Associate Director of Curatorial Affairs, The Block Museum of Art, Northwestern University, in collaboration with Candace M. Keller, Associate Professor, Art History & Visual Culture, Michigan State University.  We are grateful to the Archive of Malian Photography (https://amp.matrix.msu.edu/), which provided digital scans of archival works. At The Block, the exhibition is supported by the Kadin/Spiegel Family Endowed Fund and a grant from the Illinois Arts Council.

title: Teresa Montoya’s Tó Łitso (Yellow Water): Ten Years after the Gold King Mine Spill
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
On August 5, 2015, the rupture of the abandoned Gold King Mine near Silverton, Colorado, released more than three million gallons of toxic wastewater into the Animas River, turning its waters a shocking shade of yellow. In the following year, artist and anthropologist Teresa Montoya (Diné, born 1984) embarked on a road trip from Silverton to Shiprock, New Mexico, retracing the path of the contaminated water and documenting its ongoing cultural, spiritual, and material effects on the Navajo Nation and other Indigenous communities downstream.
Marking the ten-year anniversary of the disaster, The Block Museum of Art partners with Montoya to revisit this journey. Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) explores the enduring consequences of the Gold King Mine spill through photography, sound recordings, water samples, and cartographic data.
Combining documentary photographs with scientific data and poetic reflection, Tó Łitso invites viewers to consider water not only as a life-sustaining resource but also as a conduit for histories, stories, and harm. In doing so, the exhibition challenges extractive frameworks of land use, centering Indigenous knowledge and resilience. Through Montoya’s interdisciplinary practice, Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) offers a powerful meditation on environmental and cultural justice in the Southwest and beyond.
"The 2015 spill had discharged more than three million gallons of acidic mine waste fluid into Cement Creek before joining the Animas River and eventually into the San Juan River, which flows directly across the Navajo Nation. Through my photographic journey, I make explicit the enduring presence of toxicity across multiple landscapes and territories. Sometimes it appears beautiful, other times haunting. The images highlight the relationships that various communities sustain through water/tó despite the occurrence of repeated and enduring contamination from upstream locales. The Gold King Mine Spill shows us this, even in that, which cannot be readily seen." – Teresa Montoya

title: Xuefei Yang, guitar
location: Patrick G. and Shirley W. Ryan Center for the Musical Arts, 70 Arts Circle, Evanston, IL
description:
Part of the Segovia Classical Guitar Series
“with sparkle, clarity, and flair.”
—New York Sun
Xuefei Yang is acclaimed as one of the world’s finest classical guitarists. Hailed as a musical pioneer, Yang was the first guitarist in China to enter a music conservatory and become internationally recognized for the instrument. Yang has played in over fifty countries at major venues and festivals, both in recital and with the world’s leading orchestras. Gramophone praised Yang as one of the leading innovators of her generation for continuing to build the guitar canon; she frequently commissions new works, makes her own arrangements, and adds Chinese music and chamber music to her repertoire. Yang has played on over 30 recordings, with her second album earning “Editor’s Choice” from Gramophone. Her collaborations have included musicians such as Ian Bostridge, the Heath Quartet, and David Garrett. The UK’s Classic FM named Yang one of the top 100 classical musicians of our time, and she was awarded a Fellowship of the Royal Academy of Music in 2012. Since 2015, Yang has served as artistic director of the Changsha International Guitar Festival.
Segovia Classical Guitar Series new subscriptions and single tickets are available now.

title: Thriving Through Menopause
description: Menopause is a natural transition, but that doesn’t mean you have to suffer through it. Kristin Dold Bennett, DO, FACOG, leads a practical, evidence-based conversation about perimenopause and menopause. She'll address hot flashes, brain fog, sleep disruption, libido changes, and treatment options tailored to individual needs. Walk away informed, empowered, and confident.Dr. Bennett is a board-certified OB-GYN at Midwest Center for Women’s HealthCare in Skokie and Buffalo Grove. She provides comprehensive women’s health care, including preventive gynecology, annual exams, contraception counseling, prenatal care, and menopause management. She is dedicated to personalized, patient-centered obstetric and gynecologic care for women at every stage of life. Learn more at https://mcwhc.com/providers/dr-kristie-dold-bennett-obgyn/Keyword: Health & Wellness

title: Writers Fest 2026
location: Deering Library, 1937 Sheridan Road, Evanston, IL
description:
Please join us for the 18th annual Writers Festival, featuring renowned writers offering public readings!
This year's featured writers are Leni Zumas, Aminatta Forna, and Marsha de la O.
May 11: Reading by Leni Zumas at 5pm.
Leni Zumas is the author of four books of fiction, including Wolf Bells (Algonquin/Little, Brown, 2025). Her bestselling novel Red Clocks won the 2019 Oregon Book Award for Fiction and was shortlisted for the Orwell Prize for Political Fiction and the Neukom Award for Speculative Fiction.
May 12: Reading by Aminatta Forna at 5pm.
Aminatta Forna is the award-winning author of the novels Happiness, The Hired Man, The Memory of Love and Ancestor Stones, and a memoir, The Devil that Danced on the Water. Her latest book, an essay collection, The Window Seat: Notes from a Life in Motion, was published in 2021.
May 13: Reading by Marsha de la O at 5pm.
Marsha de la O was born and raised in Southern California. She earned her MFA from Vermont College and is the author of two collections of poetry: Black Hope (1997), winner of the New Issues Poetry Prize, and Antidote for Night (2015), winner of the Isabella Gardner Prize from BOA Editions.

title: Movie Night
description: Agent of HappinessBhutan measures happiness via the Gross National Happiness (GNH) index. Amber, a happiness agent working for the government, surveys people across the country, asking questions to ascertain their happiness levels. Documentarians Arun Bhattarai and Dorottya Zurbó follow Amber not only through his work and the diverse people he interviews, but also through his personal life. The result is a touching and thought-provoking exploration of happiness and sadness, dreams and reality, prosperity and hardship, companionship and loneliness, as well as a subtle scrutiny of the concept of GNH. Directed by Arun Bhattarai and Dorottya Zurbó.After watching the film, share your questions and comments in a discussion with Chris and Sharon.2024 : 94 minutes : Not rated: DocumentaryIn Nepali and Dzongkha with English subtitles.Free tickets will be given to those who attend.Watch the trailer for Agent of Happiness.Find Agent of Happiness in the library catalog.

title: Evaluating the Pretrial Reforms in Illinois’ SAFE-T Act
description: This event is hosted by The League of Women Voters Winnetka-Northfield Kenilworth. Visit my.lwv.org/illinois/winnetka-northfield-kenilworth for more information.This presentation will provide an overview of the changes made to Illinois’ pretrial practices through the Safety, Accountability, Fairness and Equity-Today (SAFE-T) Act, along with findings from a large-scale, long-term evaluation of the implementation and impact of these changes across Illinois being conducted by Loyola University Chicago’s Center for Criminal Justice. Dr. David Olson, Professor and co-Director of Loyola’s Center for Criminal Justice, will be the presenter. Prior to his position at Loyola, David worked as a senior scientist at the Illinois Criminal Justice Information Authority. During his nearly 40 year career in the field of criminal justice, Dr. Olson has worked with a variety of federal, state, and local agencies, and numerous task forces and commissions, to develop and evaluate criminal justice programs and policies. Dr. Olson currently serves as chair of the advisory boards of the Illinois Department of Corrections, the Illinois Department of Juvenile Justice, and the Illinois Criminal Justice Information Authority, and is a member of the Illinois Supreme Court’s Judicial College Committee on Pretrial Education and the Illinois Department of Human Services Firearm Violence Research Group. Dr. Olson received his B.S. in Criminal Justice from Loyola University Chicago, his M.A. in Criminal Justice and his Ph.D. in Political Science/Public Policy Analysis, both from the University of Illinois at Chicago.

title: Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
The storied city of Djenné, a center of Islamic learning, study, and scholarship since the twelfth century, is the hometown of Bamako-based photographer Hamdia Traoré (b. 1992, Mali). The thirty portraits in Traore’s series Des marabouts de Djenné (Marabouts of Jenne) reflect his intimate connections to the city’s people and deep history. Learned and devout, marabouts teach in Djenné’s over 50 Qur’anic schools, offer spiritual guidance, and treat ailments through their knowledge of the Qur’an.
Made during a time of political and social upheaval in Mali, these portraits reflect enduring cultural resilience. Each image depicts a marabout seated with the tools of his practice—books, Qur’an boards, amulets, and prayer beads—framed by the architecture and atmosphere of Djenné. The consistent format underscores their collective identity, while individual poses and captions highlight personal roles and neighborhoods.
Traoré’s work will be shown alongside mid-20th-century black-and-white portraits of marabouts by Malian photographers Mamadou Cissé, Abdourahmane Sakaly, and Tijani Sitou. Drawn from the Archive of Malian Photography, these earlier images share visual parallels and deepen the historical context. Together, these images illuminate evolving perspectives on spiritual authority, identity, and visual representation in Mali.

Hamdia Traoré’s “Des marabouts de Djenné” and Muslim Portraiture in Mali” is curated by Kathleen Bickford Berzock, Associate Director of Curatorial Affairs, The Block Museum of Art, Northwestern University, in collaboration with Candace M. Keller, Associate Professor, Art History & Visual Culture, Michigan State University.  We are grateful to the Archive of Malian Photography (https://amp.matrix.msu.edu/), which provided digital scans of archival works. At The Block, the exhibition is supported by the Kadin/Spiegel Family Endowed Fund and a grant from the Illinois Arts Council.

title: Teresa Montoya’s Tó Łitso (Yellow Water): Ten Years after the Gold King Mine Spill
location: Block Museum of Art, Mary and Leigh, 40 Arts Circle Drive, Evanston, IL
description:
On August 5, 2015, the rupture of the abandoned Gold King Mine near Silverton, Colorado, released more than three million gallons of toxic wastewater into the Animas River, turning its waters a shocking shade of yellow. In the following year, artist and anthropologist Teresa Montoya (Diné, born 1984) embarked on a road trip from Silverton to Shiprock, New Mexico, retracing the path of the contaminated water and documenting its ongoing cultural, spiritual, and material effects on the Navajo Nation and other Indigenous communities downstream.
Marking the ten-year anniversary of the disaster, The Block Museum of Art partners with Montoya to revisit this journey. Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) explores the enduring consequences of the Gold King Mine spill through photography, sound recordings, water samples, and cartographic data.
Combining documentary photographs with scientific data and poetic reflection, Tó Łitso invites viewers to consider water not only as a life-sustaining resource but also as a conduit for histories, stories, and harm. In doing so, the exhibition challenges extractive frameworks of land use, centering Indigenous knowledge and resilience. Through Montoya’s interdisciplinary practice, Tó Łitso (Yellow Water) offers a powerful meditation on environmental and cultural justice in the Southwest and beyond.
"The 2015 spill had discharged more than three million gallons of acidic mine waste fluid into Cement Creek before joining the Animas River and eventually into the San Juan River, which flows directly across the Navajo Nation. Through my photographic journey, I make explicit the enduring presence of toxicity across multiple landscapes and territories. Sometimes it appears beautiful, other times haunting. The images highlight the relationships that various communities sustain through water/tó despite the occurrence of repeated and enduring contamination from upstream locales. The Gold King Mine Spill shows us this, even in that, which cannot be readily seen." – Teresa Montoya

title: Writers Fest 2026
location: Deering Library, 1937 Sheridan Road, Evanston, IL
description:
Please join us for the 18th annual Writers Festival, featuring renowned writers offering public readings!
This year's featured writers are Leni Zumas, Aminatta Forna, and Marsha de la O.
May 11: Reading by Leni Zumas at 5pm.
Leni Zumas is the author of four books of fiction, including Wolf Bells (Algonquin/Little, Brown, 2025). Her bestselling novel Red Clocks won the 2019 Oregon Book Award for Fiction and was shortlisted for the Orwell Prize for Political Fiction and the Neukom Award for Speculative Fiction.
May 12: Reading by Aminatta Forna at 5pm.
Aminatta Forna is the award-winning author of the novels Happiness, The Hired Man, The Memory of Love and Ancestor Stones, and a memoir, The Devil that Danced on the Water. Her latest book, an essay collection, The Window Seat: Notes from a Life in Motion, was published in 2021.
May 13: Reading by Marsha de la O at 5pm.
Marsha de la O was born and raised in Southern California. She earned her MFA from Vermont College and is the author of two collections of poetry: Black Hope (1997), winner of the New Issues Poetry Prize, and Antidote for Night (2015), winner of the Isabella Gardner Prize from BOA Editions.

title: Own the Interview: Preparation That Reduces Anxiety
description: Interviews feel uncertain, and uncertainty creates anxiety. This workshop shows how focused preparation reduces both. You’ll build a story vault of accomplishments to make your value clear; create confident first lines with powerful, intentional language; and sketch a simple 90-day plan that signals your readiness. You'll learn to apply powerful, intentional language so that you start your answers with confidence and how to implement a simple system with a workbook to stay consistent and in control.Presented on Zoom in partnership with the Lake Cook Career Collaborative. The presentation will be recorded and available for viewing afterward. Register and receive a Zoom link.

title: Babel-On with R.F. Kuang
description: R.F. Kuang’s genre-bending fiction broaches ordinarily serious topics from a satirical and fantastical perspective. Academic yet approachable, her work combines history, magic, and classical literary tradition to render powerful critiques of academia, the publishing industry, and even contemporary popular culture. Her most recent novel, Katabasis, follows two graduate students as they descend into hell after the death of their professor. The screen rights options to the novel were sold to Amazon MGM Studios for an upcoming TV series before its publication. Her other bestselling titles include Yellowface, Babel, and The Poppy War trilogy, and she is the recipient of the Nebula Award for Best Novel, the Goodreads Choice Awards for Best Fiction, and the American Book Award. Kelly Jensen, an anti-censorship advocate, a senior editor at Book Riot, and writer who has compiled such anthologies as Here We Are: Feminism for the Real World and (Don’t) Call Me Crazy, will be leading the conversation.This event is made possible by Illinois Libraries Present (ILP), a statewide collaboration among public libraries offering premier events. ILP is funded in part by a grant awarded by the Illinois State Library, a department of the Office of Secretary of State, using funds provided by the U.S. Institute of Museum and Library Services, under the provisions of the Library Services and Technology Act. ILP is committed to inclusion and accessibility. To request accommodations, please email illinoislibrariespresent@ila.org. Register and receive a Zoom link for this event.

title: Between the Lines: The Mighty Red by Louise Erdrich
location: 6140 Lincoln Avenue, Morton Grove, IL, 60053, US
description: Between the Lines is now held on two different days, either in person or on Zoom. Titles are always available on Hoopla, as well as other library ebook platforms. New members are warmly welcome! Book Synopsis: In this stunning novel, Pulitzer Prize and National Book Award–winning author Louise Erdrich tells a story of love, natural forces, spiritual yearnings, and the tragic impact of uncontrollable circumstances on ordinary people’s lives.In the Red River Valley of North Dakota, several lives revolve around a wedding fraught with desire, jealousy, and uncertainty. Gary Geist, a terrified young man set to inherit two farms, is desperate to marry Kismet Poe, an impulsive, lapsed goth who can’t read her own future but will settle for fulfilling his. Her best friend, Hugo, a gentle, red-haired, homeschooled giant, also loves Kismet and is determined to steal her away and build a life together. Kismet’s mother, Crystal, drives a truck for Gary’s family, and on her nightly runs, tunes in to the darkness of late-night radio, experiences visions of guardian angels, and worries about what’s to come, for her daughter and herself.The Mighty Red is Louise Erdrich at her consummate best. A novel of tender humor, disquietude, yearning, community, and family, it is about ordinary people who dream, grow up, fall in love, struggle, endure tragedy, carry bitter secrets; men and women both complicated and contradictory, flawed and decent, lonely and hopeful. Human time, deep time, Red River time, and geological time are explored alongside the impact of crises in our own time—climate change, the depletion of natural resources, the economic meltdown of 2008. It is a story about our tattered bond with the earth, and about love in all of its absurdity and splendor.

title: Friday Film
description: HamnetThis lush, emotional historical drama—winner of the Golden Globe for Best Drama—is based on the critically acclaimed novel by Maggie O'Farrell, who cowrote the screenplay with Oscar-winning director Chloé Zhao. Set in 16th-century Tudor England, the film follows Agnes, a gifted healer, and her husband Will, whose marriage is pushed to the breaking point by the sudden death of their 11-year-old son, Hamnet, from the plague. Agnes grapples with a raw grief that threatens to consume her, while Will ultimately pours his profound sorrow into the creation of his immortal masterpiece, Hamlet.Starring Jessie Buckley, Paul Mescal, Emily Watson, and Joe Alwyn. Directed by Chloé Zhao. Hamnet received eight Academy Award nominations, including Best Picture, Director, Actress (Buckley), and Adapted Screenplay. 2025 : 126 minutes : Rated PG-13 for thematic content, some strong sexuality, and partial nudity : Historical dramaFree tickets will be given to those who attend.Watch a trailer for Hamnet.Find Hamnet in the library catalog.

title: Morning Movie
description: Elio Eleven-year-old Elio believes that extraterrestrials are out there and feels he’d fit in better with them than with people. One day, his dream comes true. He's taken to the Communiverse and mistaken for an ambassador from Earth. This fun action-packed cosmic misadventure explores themes of loneliness, belonging, grief, loss, family, acceptance, self-discovery, and identity. Elio and his space-alien best friend Glordon are endearing and relatable, and the animation is bright and imaginative. Featuring the voice talents of Yonas Kibreab, Remy Edgerly, Zoe Saldana, Brad Garrett, Jameela Jamil, Shirley Henderson, and Matthias Schweighofer. Directed by Madeline Sharafian, Domee Shi, and Adrian Molina. This film received an Oscar nomination for Best Animated Feature Film. 2025 : 98 minutes : Rated PG for some action/peril and thematic elements : AnimationFree tickets will be given to all who attend. Watch the trailer for Elio.Find Elio in the library catalog.

title: Pages Through History
description: This month we'll be discussing The Second Founding: How the Civil War and Reconstruction Remade the Constitution by Eric Foner. It would be hard for a person contemplating the United States of 1865 not to conclude that the system the Founders had bequeathed the nation just under a century earlier had been catastrophically flawed. Pulitzer Prize-winning historian Eric Foner lays out the ways the 13th, 14th, and 15th Amendments–all passed in just five years–represented an attempt to fix those flaws. They abolished slavery, codified the principles of birthright citizenship and legal equality, and guaranteed the right of Black men to vote. We live in the America the “Reconstruction Amendments” made, but we’ve been fighting about those amendments ever since.   Find The Second Founding in the library catalog.

title: Morning Movie
description: We Bought a ZooBenjamin Mee, recently widowed with two children—14-year-old Dylan and 7-year-old Rosie—thinks that starting over will do them all good, especially after Dylan gets expelled from school. While looking to relocate, they find a dilapidated old house that happens to come with a zoo that needs saving. There, they meet zookeeper Kelly Foster and her small, dedicated staff, who want to see it reopened. Despite the huge financial commitment and having no experience, Benjamin takes on the task with the help of his family.Loosely inspired by a true story, this is a healing adventure about second chances. Starring Matt Damon, Scarlett Johansson, Thomas Haden Church, Patrick Fugit, and Elle Fanning. title: Movie Night
description: Queen of GloryWriter-director-actor Nana Mensah does triple duty in this film. The story centers on Sarah Obeng (Mensah), a Ghanaian American PhD student in molecular neuro-oncology at Columbia University. She is about to relocate to Ohio with her married boyfriend when news comes that her mother has died unexpectedly. Sarah returns to the Bronx to settle her affairs, which include inheriting a Christian bookstore called King of Glory, and she finds herself navigating the expectations of Ghanaian relatives and the community while figuring out what she wants and needs. Starring Nana Mensah, Meeko Gattuso, Oberon K.A. Adjepong, Adam Leon, Russell G. Jones, and Anya Migdal. description: A Nice Indian BoyNaveen, a successful doctor, has always dreamed of having a Bollywood-style wedding like his sister’s. His guarded nature, however, has kept him from finding Mr. Right. title: Songs of the Illinois Freedom Road
description: Award-winning folk musician and folklorist Chris Vallillo brings the music and the stories of the Underground Railroad in Illinois to life in this engaging and powerful show featuring first-person accounts of Freedom Seekers as well as performances and insights into the music that drove this historic movement. “While traditional accounts of the underground railroad generally dismiss Illinois, our state had more miles bordering slave states than any other” said Chris, “and those borders were rivers which made for excellent escape routes.”Chris Vallillo is a singer/songwriter and roots musician who makes the people and places of “unmetropolitan” America come to life in song. Having spent the last 30 years in the rural Midwest, he has a natural affinity for American roots music. He weaves original, contemporary, and traditional songs and narratives into a compelling and entertaining portrait of the history and lifestyles of the Midwest.Free tickets will be distributed to those present at 2:30 pm.

title: Juneteenth holiday Observance, College closed
description: Oakton College is closed in observance of Juneteenth.

title: Monarch Community Science Project
description: Monarch butterflies play an important role as pollinators, and their populations are in trouble. Teen volunteers will learn about them and teach others, too.Tend our native garden patch, observe monarch eggs and caterpillars, and talk with other library patrons about your observations and how we can all support monarchs. Data will be submitted to the Monarch Larva Monitoring Project. All participants receive service hours as part of Teen Summer Service. All participants receive service hours as part of Teen Summer Service. Fill out this contact form to be added to our list of interested teen volunteers and be one of the first to know about volunteer opportunities.Email the teen team to learn more or to express an interest in volunteering. For grade 6-12.

title: Home Care and Bingo for Adults 55+
description: Navigating home care options can be challenging. Learn more about the types of home care with representatives from Serenity Home Healthcare. After the presentation, stay for bingo and an optional blood pressure screening.Light refreshments will be provided.

title: Cardboard Creators
description: Transform ordinary cardboard and boxes into extraordinary creations inspired by your favorite books. Listen to a story and then bring it to life with cardboard and your own two hands!  Free tickets will be given to all who attend. Kids younger than eight must have an adult in the library during the event.
